text:
A cold light fills the viewscreen, stark against the utter darkness of the hyperspace abyss. It seems to pulse, shifting dimmer and then brighter, following no discernable pattern. It does not flicker like a flame, nor like a failing comms signal, but like... something at the edge of your consciousness. It is...

You find you are able to anticipate the rhythm, more seen than heard, the dancing light filling your vision...

A polite cough from your sensors officer brings you back to the present. "$PlayerSirOrMadam?"

text:
The cold light fills the viewscreen, stark against the abyss. The subtle pulse of the light begs for attention, but gives no promise of comfort.

You give your head half a shake, centering your attention on your bridge officers to give your next order.

options: abyssalLight_what:"Sensors, what are we looking at?"

text:
"It's an abyssal light, $playerSirOrMadam."

Your sensors officer quickly realizes that this statement is as obvious as it is unilluminating.

"And. We don't know exactly what it... is. There's fragmentary pre-Collapse data. Some academicians speculate that it might be a spontaneous buildup of dark energy, maybe, around a temporary hyperspace quasistructure, a sort of knot in higher dimensions that alters the physical effects of... whatever it is."

You catch your ops chief making the spacer hand-sign for 'all seals confirmed safe', a sort of ward against evil.

script:
AddTextSmall "    - Use an Active Sensor Burst to increase sensor range
    - Use an Interdiction Pulse to increase burn level
    - Lasts for a certain number of light-years traveled
    - Larger increase when used near the Abyssal Light" highlight
$player.gotFirstAbyssalLightInfodump = true
FireAll AbyssalLightOptions
text:
"They're not known, um, to be dangerous," your sensors officer continues, tapping at the console. "The drive field should keep us safe."

"It does appear to be highly unstable. They always are, and they're difficult to properly scan. I think if we tried an active scan or fired off an interdiction pulse it would collapse; the resulting discharge might allow us to chart local hyperspace topography."

Your ops chief, normally unflappable, almost flinches.

"$PlayerSirOrMadam. No, $playerSirOrMadam."

You let the silence linger, letting ops know that denial is not enough of an answer.

"Captain." Ops looks at you directly. "It's said to be... bad luck to meddle with things this deep in hyperspace." Ops stands up straighter, "I've no technical opinion; I raise the point only as a matter of crew morale. Mere dockside superstition, I'm sure."
options:
abyssalLight_askOpsOutA:"Understood, I'll be mindful. Thank you chief."
abyssalLight_askOpsOutB:"Such superstition has no place on my bridge, is that understood?"

text: "$PlayerSirOrMadam," your ops chief says, then turns back to the main display, determined to make nothing more of it.
"Understood, $playerSirOrMadam." Your ops chief turns back to the main display, determined to make nothing more of it.

Cold light fills the primary display, stark against the abyss. The subtle pulse begs for your attention yet offers no promise of comfort.

After a moment you consciously pull your eyes from the pitiless sight, centering your attention on your bridge officers to give your next order.

Cold light begins to grow in the primary display. A subtle pulse begs for your attention.

"Captain, I-" It's your navigation officer, who glances at sensors and receives a nod. "The light is moving. Coming closer."

You consciously pull your eyes from that comfortless light - diminished but not bereft of its call through even the meager medium of the primary display.

It's come to you.

"$PlayerSirOrMadam!" Your sensors officer's voice is strained. "Picking up - from the light - they came from out of nowhere!" 

Navigation interrupts, "$ShipOrFleet drive bubble in the red, instability increasing... holding... alert to multiple penetrations. N-space fissures detected inside the volume."

A pause as the bridge crew takes in the eruption of alerts on their displays. Tactical takes initiative and activates the combat alarm to send all crew to battle stations. Frantic activity from all posts, chatter between the stations coordinating response.

Sensors calls over the low din: "It's homing in on our active sensor emissions."
options:
abyssalLight_maxSensors:Order a sensor sweep, full power, focused on the anomalous drive-bubble penetrations
abyssalLight_wait:Attendre
abyssalLight_emCon:"Go to maximum emission control! EMCON zero-zero alpha, immediate execute."

text:
"Aye $playerSirOrMadam! Executing." Sensors barks, then deftly executes a series of overrides. 

Your $shipOrFleet status is up on the central display - shield bubble an unsteady amber, incursions of N-space fissures marked in garish red. A blink of white as active sensors trigger and-

-pressure on the drive bubble spikes, an amber flash, a cacophony of alarms...

* * *

A hidden blackness waiting, lurking, jolts awake. Tendrils lunge eagerly, hungrily, through fissures in the dark behind the night.

At your command, the unperceived chorus of a multitude of ship systems and machinery dies down with a deepening sigh into muted quietude.

All that remains is the breath and bodies of your bridge crew, a strange assertion of your shared humanity. 

Backup e-lighting, simple chemical things, provide an eerie glow. A series of dimly pulsing commands illuminate your executive interface - a flock of ardent subsystems ready to awaken from minimum operation at your touch.

* * *

A hidden blackness feels the brush of a shadow. Less than a morsel, unworthy of wakefulness. By some analog of distributed instinct a few listless tendrils explore this disturbance, this ripple in the darkness behind the night.

You wait.

Alarms are reset, battle restraints engaged. Activity on the bridge gradually quiets as all officers, crew, and systems reach a state of readiness, even anticipation - fight or flight.

The incursions crawl closer to the center of your display volume, expanding unwaveringly and intentionally. 

All eyes are on command interfaces, and all attention is on your next order.

script:
ShowImageVisual shrouded_weapon_assembly
SetStoryOption shroudedSubstrateBegin 1 analyzeSubstrate technology "Analyzed Shrouded Substrate"
text:
You join your technology officer and engineering chief in a small briefing room overlooking the shop bay dedicated to what has been euphemistically dubbed "special salvage".

Haz-suited technicians attend to the containment machinery, stepping over coils of tube and wire. A mess of makeshift components were pulled from the common microforge libraries then torn apart and re-engineered to operate in this unusual, even extraordinary, application.

Engineering wears a frown, tapping a TriPad as they observe the ongoing work. Tech can barely contain their excitement. 

"$PlayerSirOrMadam," they say in unison at your arrival.
notes: # The idea that the PC would call a meeting, show up, and then leave without a word is legitimately funny to me. -dgb

text:
"Exotic matter!" Tech exclaims.

Engineering interrupts. "Another way of saying 'we don't know what it is', and 'it doesn't act how physics says it should'." 

Your technology officer begins again, enthusiasm tempered - somewhat. "Obviously, I was about to- $playerSirOrMadam," Tech turns to face only you, perhaps to curb further interruptions. "Analysis is preliminary and proceeding," a quick glimpse at the frowning engineering chief, "carefully."

options: shroudedSubstrate_optSelAga2:"The Galatia Academy would be interested, I think."
shroudedSubstrate_optSelF:"Show me what you can do."

text:
Your engineering chief makes a show of looking to Tech expectantly, mouth quite intentionally shut. It appears they've had this argument already.

"As, um." Tech passes a look of consternation back toward your chief engineer.

"In truth, I could not say, exactly. And unfortunately we're not equipped to perform a full scientific investigation. Clearly it is a part of, or a product of the, the... hostile force which we bested. But to know what it IS... you'd need a full science team and laboratory facilities to even begin to approach the right questions, to say nothing of answers..."
options:
shroudedSubstrate_optSelAga:"Like the Galatia Academy."
shroudedSubstrate_optSelAtt:"Like Tri-Tachyon."

text: "Yes, exactly." Tech nods. "Although..."

Your engineering chief answers. "Yes, there's nothing like this in the public archives. The Galatia Academy is supposed to be neutral. But as you know, $playerSirOrMadam, the Hegemony will reach in to put its hands on the controls, so to speak, if it sees something it doesn't like. The Hegemony doesn't like disruption. And this..."

A hand is extended to indicate the contents of the workbay.

Tech nods agreement, "The Ministry of Technology Standards would surely seize such specimens. I could not say what they would do with them, but we would not see them ever again. That much is certain."

Your engineering chief answers. "Yes, there's nothing like this in the public archives. The Galatia Academy is supposed to be neutral. But as you know, $playerSirOrMadam, the Hegemony will reach in to put its hands on the controls, so to speak, if it sees something it doesn't like. The Hegemony doesn't like disruption. And this..."

A hand is extended to indicate the contents of the workbay.

Tech nods agreement, "The Ministry of Technology Standards would surely seize such specimens. I could not say what they would do with them, but we would not see them ever again. That much is certain."
Tech nods, "Yes, their facilities would be more than able to perform this research. And," a smile, "they are likely to pay very well, if what they say is true."
options:
shroudedSubstrate_optSelAttA:"What exactly do 'they' say?"
shroudedSubstrate_optSelAttB:"Who is this 'they'?"

text: "The Company pays very well indeed for unique specimens," Tech explains. "Artifacts; Domain tech, xenobiology. Anything unknown to the present Persean Sector, anything strange which might demonstrate uncommon principles or serve an unexpected use."

"Independent salvors, scavengers, fringe surveyors," Tech explains.

"Those sorts of people who might find artifacts, specimens; Domain tech, xenobiology. Anything unknown to the present Persean Sector, anything strange which might demonstrate uncommon principles or serve unexpected use. Tri-Tachyon makes it known that great rewards will be paid if they are brought 'examples of special interest'."

"Extremely," Tech answers, exchanging a look with your chief engineer. "If the energy output ratio scales as projected..."

"It's dangerous," Engineering says. "It's almost too easy to build a weapon with this 'substrate'. Imagine if the Pathers-"

"-Yes, yes, another Mairaath," Tech interrupts. "As with any applied technology, sufficiently powerful, it could... $playerSirOrMadam. To answer your query, yes, extremely valuable."

"Very," your technology officer exchanges another look with Engineering. "In my professional opinion."

"When stabilized, and... provoked, it appears to create energy from... somewhere else. Or transform it, enhance it. The gain is spectacular."

Your chief engineer steps forward. "That test with the laser-cutter would have blown a hole through the hull if," a glance at Tech, "if we were not following protocol by using a blast-plate backing in the test zone."

"Look, I did concede you were correct to-" Tech catches your expression and shuts up, putting on a deferential look.

Engineering smirks at Tech.

"It is essentially unstable, if left alone," Tech admits. "Without containment, it will," a pause, searching for the word. 

"Fade away? No, withdraw. Slowly. The process is non-linear, and appears to accelerate, given time. Although it's hard to measure exact terms of mass, volume, or... output. It all just becomes... less."

Tech looks to Engineering, who can barely suppress a grin despite a previous air of skepticism. Your chief engineer nods, and flips a TriPad around.

"It's pretty simple, $playerSirOrMadam. Scale up the testing rig using modified common equipment to produce outputs in the combat application range. A robust chassis is not difficult. We're already equipped to repair modular components of most weapon systems using microforged components and existing supply stocks." 

They pause, looking into the workbay once more. "The key is this 'substrate'. It provides the core, the... amplification, the transformation."

Tech speaks up, "Using sensor data recorded during our encounter with the, um, hostile entities, we believe it is possible to reliably reproduce certain effects which have been observed."

The schematics scrolling before you are complex, but you recognize the conduits and capacitors common to energy weapon mounts. And something strange at the center of each design, some beating heart which does not belong within these human creations.

You trust your officers enough not to ask if the projected power outputs are an error - you've already seen what these things are capable of.

"$PlayerSirOrMadam," they say once more in unison.

Shrouded Substrate available: $player.shroudedSubstrateAvailable unit(s).

You consult with your technical officer and engineering chief in a small briefing room overlooking a workbay adapted to store and analyze the unusual specimens you've found. They are poring over schematics, deep in an argument over implementation details. You are pleased to note that their disagreement plays out within a foundation built of professional respect.  

They notice you and, in unison, say "$PlayerSirOrMadam."

Engineering is frowning, manipulating a diagram of a ship with abrupt flicks at the interface. "It'd have to be anchored to the flux grid. Only way to keep it wrapped close enough to the hull geometry."

"Precisely," Tech says. "To keep the exotic material from diminishing! It will draw energy from incoming projectiles, storing it in the, um... meta-plasma?"

"We can't store energy forever in this theoretical, pretend-plasma," Engineering says. "It'll have to go somewhere."

You find your technology officer and chief engineer in another deep discussion over one of the unusual specimens held in the "special salvage' bay.

"This 'lens' - a working term, of course - does not operate in normal space as-such."

Tech is deep in lecture mode over an arcane holo-diagram. You can tell they've been talking for some minutes from Engineering's impatient eye roll.

"It could be emplaced deep within the hull, in a specially stabilized cradle - for safety," Tech continues.

Engineering appears pained at describing this proposal as 'safe'.

Tech proceeds without acknowledging any objection.

"-spin it up during combat maneuvers, adjusting the frequency of the 'probes' adapted from drive-field stabilizers. It would create hyperspace fractures which would cause terrible damage to n-space structures. Starships, for instance. Now, for obvious reasons, the theoretical range is infinite..."
options: ShroudedHML_talk4:"A weapon with infinite range, you say?"

text:
Both jump at your words, standing to attention. "$PlayerSirOrMadam!" they say in uncharacteristic unison.

Engineering takes the opportunity to finally get a word in. "Accuracy decreases in... interestingly, a sharp harmonic progression. But this means anything beyond mid-range combat parameters will effectively create an energetic burst at a random spot somewhere within the n-space universe." They side-eye Tech. "Just another blip of cosmic background radiation."

Tech looks like they might say something, but no words come. Engineering fills the silence again. "Practically, it could only target reasonably nearby hostiles."

You consider the undeniable possibility that the Luddic faith would view any use of this technology as literally demonic. You could be declared a heretical outlaw and hunted by the Knights of Ludd and Luddic Path alike.

id: # Rogue giant planet in abyssal space; turbulence in atmosphere

text:
Your $shipOrFleet approaches $entityName.

The huge planet spins languidly below your $shipOrFleet, deep in a cryosleep of its own between the far-flung stars of the galaxy. Gravitational compression and the barest trickle of radioactive decay warm the interior well above the interstellar medium, feeding active weather systems which slowly churn across the cold face of the world.

"$PlayerSirOrMadam, I-" your sensors officer pulls up the live feed from a sensor subsuite. This gets the attention of Tactical.

After a moment, even you can see it: a disruption, a gout of sudden turbulence like a dozen giant hurricanes turning over.

"Massive storm system. Sudden heat anomaly, um, relatively speaking. Some kind of upwelling," your sensors officer responds, rapid-fire. You know they don't like not having a clear answer.

"Powering up gravimetrics. If there's something hiding under those clouds, that'll show it."

They tap at their console, calibrating a new subsuite of the $shipOrFleet sensor network. The minutes tick by as the systems self-adjust to the planet, the estimated accuracy of readings steadily rising on the shared display. The readout sharpens, clarifies, begins to map the mass-density of $entityName.

At the same time, the anomaly subsides, leaving storm-cells the size of moons spinning through the atmosphere.

Sensors looks increasingly agitated, rapidly switching between sensor interfaces to collate telemetry.

"Captain, I cannot ascertain the cause. A huge burst of energy was released into the atmosphere, an upwelling like... the mass readings don't show any unexpected structures or objects, natural or otherwise."

Tactical frowns, ever suspicious, and says "This happens just when our $shipOrFleet arrives? I don't like it."
options:
abyssalGasGiant_turb4:"All stations at ready. Sensors, call alert if there's any change."
100:defaultLeave:"I don't like it either. Nav, get us out of here."

Below, $entityName spins complacently, its shadow-side occasionally lit with patches of lightning as titanic atmospheric currents do battle.

Your $shipOrFleet approaches $entityName.

Upon your return, your sensors officer is determined not to be caught off-guard again. The sensor net is set to high intensity and the gravimetric suite is loaded with the previously collected calibration data.

There's no sign of unusual turbulence; just the crawl of atmospheric bands, lightning storms flickering where their edges meet like battling fleets in a far-off nebula.

id: # Rogue planetoid in abyssal space; cleaned up mining OP

text:
Your $shipOrFleet approaches $entityName.

A preliminary surface scan reveals certain inconsistencies in expected mass-densities for this category of planetoid.

Your sensors officer orders additional sweeps, at higher power and resolution. A picture of the situation starts to come together: large deposits of useful elements have been extracted and the dig-sites, whether surface deposits or deep veins, have been carefully returned to something approximating their natural state. Shafts have been infilled, tailings spread evenly and packed over the extraction fields.

Sensors consults with your operations chief, who did a stint in fringe mining earlier in their career. Ops looks grim.

text:
"Don't look right, $playerSirOrMadam," Ops says, turning a weathered face to you.

"Aye, could've been an indie miner or scav. Could've. But there's nothing- no temporary works, no pillaring, no tread tracks. Should be dead power cells and ration wrappers scattered like stars in the dark. Ain't nothing."

Ops scowls. "No one cleans up like this, not even Tachy autominers. No profit in it. Especially not out here."

text:
Your $shipOrFleet approaches $entityName.

The faint signs of a massive mining operation remain and are just as suspiciously neat as before, as tidy as a penitent Luddic rock-gardener. Sensor records do not show any significant changes since the last scan.

id:
# Ghost ship around rogue abyssal stellar entity
"AI cores" variation

text: Your $shipOrFleet approaches $aOrAn $nameInText.

The outside hull is oddly pristine, as if sent on a long journey fresh from the forge without personalized outfitting and customization.

Your salvors breach the exterior and find a different story: crudely anchored power conduits and coolant tubes have been rigged haphazardly through the shafts and corridors of the ship. Safety doors are stuck open and hatches cut through to provide access to serpentine bundles of cable. It's not high-quality work; the occasional powered-down maintenance drone, stupid automatic things meant to assist human technicians, suggests the obvious culprits.

Your salvors must make numerous cuts to clear access to core ship systems. It is not dangerous, merely inconvenient. It is near the true centerpoint of the hull volume that they find a trio of AI cores, two gammas and a beta from the looks of the casings. They are unpowered, the coolant congealed in its pipes.

What looked to be a valuable if legally problematic find is a bust, however: the AI cores are hollowed out, as if perfect spheres of nothingness appeared where once cybernetic superintelligence dwelled.

The computer memory of the ship proper has no answers; it was overridden from the start and appears to be convinced that it's still in its forge-cradle.
options:
salExplore:Assess the ship's salvage value
defaultLeave:Partir

text: Your $shipOrFleet approaches $aOrAn $nameInText.

Your salvors detect a very weak energy shield still active, despite main power loss. It is oddly fluctuating, as if tuned to a frequency which would be of no use in combat.

It is a simple matter to disrupt and breach the shield with standard civilian EM probe-and-shield kit, and the salvors do so handily.

A search reveals that this is, or rather was, a Tri-Tachyon flagged vessel. 

The crew is dead. What's left of the young executive with lead authority over the operation is still in his luxuriously outfitted personal cabin; such incidents involving the untimely discharge of an engineering-grade plasma cutter are not unknown in poorly managed repair docks, but when they occur in a ship's executive suite, pointed questions are raised.

The last survivor, suited against the apparent life support failure, is found near the jury-rigged power generator that was spliced into the ship's shield-grid. The suit's safeties were overridden and its atmo mix of oxygen reduced to zero.

It would have been a painless slide into unconsciousness and, soon enough, death.

"THEY FOLLOW" was scraped onto the decking with a multitool. Low oxygen psychosis is not unheard of under such catastrophic conditions.

What exactly it was this shield was supposed to keep out - or in - is unclear.
options:
salExplore:Assess the ship's salvage value
defaultLeave:Partir

text: Your $shipOrFleet approaches $aOrAn $nameInText.
Your salvors perform an orderly breach under the supervision of your ops chief, identifying and disabling several custom-rigged defensive measures.

script:
$explored = true
UniqueEncounter setInteractedWith $gsType
RemoveCommodity crew 1
PlaySound hit_heavy
text:
One salvor is overly hasty and skips a step in the measured protocols that made the reputation of your ops chief.

This earns the consequences of a custom-rigged defensive measure hidden in the access panel for the cargo bay override.
Your ops chief growls, bullying the remaining salvors into paranoid compliance. Several more explosives and panel overloads are disarmed before hull access is cleared.

This is a ship of mercenary-adventurers crewed by a colorful mix of distinct personalities from worlds representative of a broad swath of the Persean Sector.

The captain was a former officer who found himself on the losing side of minor rebellion out of the Persean League. The pilot, a racer-turned-smuggler recovered from stim addiction. Former Diktat gone renegade, present at the massacre of... scion of an Eventide officer family, overbearing mother, disowned after... the engineer, the armorer, the navigator... all irrelevant details. You skim the report faster.

You can't imagine how they managed to work together. It doesn't matter; they're all dead now.
options:
salExplore:Assess the ship's salvage value
defaultLeave:Partir

id: Abyssal ghost ships, "Chartery of Opis" variation

text: Your $shipOrFleet approaches $aOrAn $nameInText.

You watch the salvage teams via vid-feed as their rig and attendant drone swarm approach the derelict.

Details from long-range optical scans are confirmed: beneath a patina of space-weathering, the hull still bears a corporate registration and the crest of the Chartery of Opis, a polity annihilated during the Askonia Crisis of cycle 181.

The only body found aboard is the former captain, sitting at his command console, desiccated eyes fixed ahead at the suffocating nothingness of deep space.

Behind the CIC, in the armored computer center, there is a Tri-Tachyon manufactured AI core interface. Just sitting there unassuming out in the open, a perfectly casual commercial hardware installation. This dates the ship back further, to before the AI Wars.

No core lies in the cradle, however. Perhaps it was taken when the ship was abandoned.

Your tech officer oversees an attempt to read the logs, to find some hint of what happened and why - but the memory banks have all been cleared by a repeating wipe program that continued until backup power faded.

A full forensic-archeological investigation might reveal answers, but you have neither the time nor equipment for such indulgence.
options:
salExplore:Assess the ship's salvage value
defaultLeave:Partir

text: Your $shipOrFleet approaches $aOrAn $nameInText.

As your salvor teams breach the hull, they discover tell-tale signs of a pirate crew: prominently-displayed hand weapons, decor as bold and brutal as it is unsophisticated - skulls and blood-red predominate, and signs of lackadaisical maintenance performed on the less interesting ship systems.

It is not a tomb-ship, however. 

By numbers, most of the crew appears to have escaped before leaving the hull derelict. A few unfortunate victims of personal violence - or parts of them - appear to have been left behind from some kind of internal conflict. The cycles since were not kind to what remained as the life support systems slowly failed.

A cursory trawl of what logs the senior officers bothered to keep suggests that this ship was part of a small pirate flotilla tracking a well-supplied independent expedition working its way through the outer fringe. That expedition spotted the pirates, then burned rapidly toward the Abyss. The pirates followed and soon lost their prey in the stultifying darkness.

With supplies and fuel dwindling and no certainty of a 'hostile resupply', the pirate leadership began to argue, and then to scrap hulls while consolidating crew onto the remaining ships - at the barrel of a gun, as necessary.

There wasn't quite enough room for everyone, it appears.
options:
salExplore:Assess the ship's salvage value
defaultLeave:Partir

text: Your $shipOrFleet approaches $aOrAn $nameInText.

As your salvors approach, some kind of proximity sensor is triggered - a faint comms transmission begins. The power system is clearly degraded and running down, causing the transmission to sputter and warble.

It's a repeating recording, not an active feed. Your comms officer does their best to clean it up, and forwards it to your command console.

A face appears, presumably the unfortunate ship's captain, his expression twisted by an appalling mixture of privation and hatred.

"...Zunya, if you ever see this, know that my last wish and curse is that you burn, and burn slowly in the radiation of a leaking reactor. I hope your eyes boil, and your blood sublimes into the vacuum as your dying body is thrown into space by the others you've betrayed. You're as heartless as-"

He goes on to describe various torments deserved due to the poor character of someone called Zunya Glamor-Rotanev. By context clues you presume her to be some kind of high ranking Tri-Tachyon executive who commissioned him to carry out a failed expedition.
options: GS_ZGR_MERC_cont4:Try to find out what Tri-Tachyon was looking for out here

text: He goes on to describe various torments deserved due to the poor character of Director of Special Acquisitions Zunya Glamor-Rotanev. It seems unlikely that she would be interested in a recording of the transmission.
options: GS_ZGR_MERC_cont4:Try to find records of what the mercenaries may have found

text:
Your ops chief gives the order, and salvage teams move to prioritize the recovery of datacores and sensor buffers.

They are efficient in their work, and soon their relays hum with recovered data. Tiny packet drones shuttle recovered hardware between the wreck and your $shipOrFleet.

Your tech officer is only frustrated by the results, however. The datacores have been wiped, memory overwritten thousands of times with hateful messages directed at Glamor-Rotanev. If these mercs found anything, if their logs had any clues, it's all been erased. If there was anything esoteric in their cargo holds, it's been dumped.

"It's all just scrap, $playerSirOrMadam," your tech officer reports, disappointed. Your ops chief grunts neutral acknowledgement; it's always scrap to them.
options:
salExplore:Assess the ship's salvage value
defaultLeave:Partir

text: Your $shipOrFleet approaches $aOrAn $nameInText.

This hull is damaged, but through the scars of impacts and stripped armor your sensors officer recognizes the crest of the Galatia Academy. This expedition was lightly and inconspicuously armed, enough to discourage casual piracy while not offending the official neutrality of the Academy.

Perhaps this was not enough.

Salvage repeaters - a common feature of high-risk Academy probes and expeditions which promise a reward for return of data - are all dead. Manually disabled, overloaded and fused or even smashed with hand-tools, from the looks of it. Clearly someone did not want them transmitting their message, or any message, in this place.

The expedition's equipment is not the best money can buy, but the sensor suites and detector arrays were obviously lovingly modified and maintained - what's left of them, anyway. They're torn apart, twisted and burnt into jagged black filaments.

Shielded datacores are recovered by salvor teams and returned for analysis in a signal-quarantined vault kept for this purpose. It does not take long for your tech officer to report results.

"Judging from logs and fabrication tags, the expedition dates to circa cycle 176," your tech officer says. "This was the flagship of a small flotilla. The rest were... lost."

"Someone... tried to erase all records. Forge-reset."

"Not quite," your tech officer suppresses a sly grin.

There's simple deletion and there's systemic erasure done to ensure that data recovery is effectively impossible. Whoever did this was not proficient enough to do the latter.
options: GS_ACADEMY_cont4:Find out what happened to the expedition

text:
Datastreams are painstakingly recovered, the high-interest combat logs prioritized by your techs. The data is lossy, and the gaps must be filled by algorithms which, used improperly, have a reputation for confidently asserting false results.

Nonetheless.

You see the black of the abyss, and the cluster of lights and icons signifying the Galatia Expedition's flotilla. The view is highly abstract, a reconstruction of a visualization necessary in not only hyperspace but the uncertain depths of the Abyss.

You speed the stream forward to the point where everything goes wrong.

The fleet approaches an Abyssal Light. A glow in the simulated display, a foggy illumination which somehow captures the cold and solipsistic loneliness of the veil beyond deep space.

The flagship comms hum with excited academicians eager to finally perform their experiments after long weeks of boredom.

The flotilla's hyperspace bubble ripples, then strains under an impossible force. Anomalous sensor readings are tagged, then...

An error pops up from your own software. 
EXTREME UNRELIABILITY. Output may not reflect accurate reconstruction of data. Continue?

The display is full of red icons, jagged warnings vectoring toward elements of the flotilla.

The chief Academician orders a full sensor burst, max power, using the integrated suite of the entire flotilla. There is a flash as EM surges; and gravimetrics pulse, hyperwave doppler, Atherton resonators...

Another error.
INTERRUPT: INSUFFICIENT DATA. Continue to next viable reconstruction?

The feed is degraded, patchy. The flotilla is gone; only the flagship remains, panicked voices on ship comms, in retreat from its previous course. The timeline show a halting record of desperate damage repairs and the crews' slow realization that they cannot make it back to civilized space.

It is a death-spiral known and feared by all spacer crews.

Systems break down, fuel and supplies dwindle. Morale breaks down. Then discipline. It unravels faster than usual with so many civilians.

You shut it off.
options:
salExplore:Assess the ship's salvage value
defaultLeave:Partir

text: Your $shipOrFleet approaches $aOrAn $nameInText.

A starship-sized metal structure spins slowly before your $shipOrFleet. It is no ship, but a regular form of asymmetric proportions with a trough-like interior. It appears to have sustained tremendous battle-damage on its convex face, which is riven with gaping holes.

A cloud of debris, presumably from the structure, hangs in the blackness like flies around a corpse.

A starship-sized metal structure spins slowly before your $shipOrFleet. It is no ship, but a modular armor section from the Onslaught Mk.I which your sensors officer readily matches to your records despite the tremendous battle-damage.

Smaller pieces of debris, presumably from the structure, hang in the blackness like flies around a corpse.

A starship-sized metal structure spins slowly before your $shipOrFleet. It is no ship, but a modular armor section which, despite tremendous battle-damage, matches the armored vambrace of the Onslaught Mk.I.

Your sensors officer builds a scan of the structure from your $shipOrFleet's sensor suite then overlays it onto the 'arm' of your own Onslaught Mk.I. The visual match is obvious despite the violence which has torn and warped the structure.

Smaller pieces of debris, presumably from the vambrace, hang in the blackness like flies around a corpse.

options: gsVambrace_salvors1:Send salvors in for a closer look
gsVambrace_sample:Take samples for Oya Tanaica, the nanoforge engineer on Asher

script:
AddTextSmall "Acquired derelict structure samples" highlight
$player.tookVambraceSamples = true
SetPersonHidden nanoforge_engineer false
FireAll gsVambraceHubOpts
text: You order material samples taken from representative sections of the structure. It is possible that something more might be learned about its provenance.

"Spectra and mass returns are consistent, mostly, with ship armor," your sensors officer reports. "No active power systems, no atmo, no habitable volumes. Temperature approaching background."

They look over their shoulder at you. "It's dead. And it's been dead for a very long time."

Sensors gives you an apologetic look. "Conditions are strange out here, $playerSirOrMadam. We'll need a closer look to find out more."

"Spectra and mass returns are consistent with original armor composition from our, ah, matching specimen," your sensors officer reports. "No active power systems, no atmo, no habitable volumes. Temperature approaching background."

They look over their shoulder at you. "It's dead, and it's been dead for a very long time."

Sensors gives you an apologetic look. "Conditions are strange out here, $playerSirOrMadam. We'll need a closer look to find out more."

You give the word.

Your operations chief nods, then barks out deployment orders to the waiting salvor teams.

The investigation proceeds smoothly. The derelict structure and its attendant debris are truly cold and dead. Such battle-damage is familiar to your salvors, and this survey even easier due to the apparent lack of live munitions and none of the usual frozen or fried vacuum-desiccated remains.

Sensing a laxity in the crews, your ops chief growls admonitions over the general comms then orders a more detailed adherence to procedure.

As reports and scans come in, a picture emerges - both a detailed holographic mapping and an understanding of what this structure is.

It is a piece of modular armor with structural attachment points, flux vent ports, and even an auxiliary sensor network, though no internal power sources.

The engineering is all recognizable as related to Domain standards, but as if from a crude half-foreign culture. Power connectors, for instance, are robustly built but oddly shaped and accompanied by an odd drift in signage iconography. It's nothing your salvors can't understand or adapt, but it is technology unlike even the oldest nanofabricated hulls and hardware designs used in the Persean Sector.
options: gsVambrace_salvors3:"Tech, what are we looking at here?"

text:
Your technology officer jumps at your words; they were apparently quite lost in thought.

"Um, $playerSirOrMadam. If I had to... in short, it's..." 

Your ops chief maintains an even expression, pointedly unreactive to this stuttering.

"It's ancient," Tech finally settles on a word. "It can only be a product of the Early Domain."

"Judging by the battle damage..." Your technology officer looks back at the specs, struggling. "There was a battle."

A look of concern grows. "It has been here for a long time, but from the debris cloud... the conditions are strange enough, but I would speculate that this is not an untouched site. It's nothing like salvagers or scrappers, they've got distinctive patterns. I couldn't say who, or what, but I suspect the site has been disturbed, or moved."

"If only it had datacores, buffers, something to analyze!" Tech looks at your ops chief, who gives a single shake of their head.

You give the word.

Your operations chief nods, then barks out deployment orders to the waiting salvor teams.

The investigation proceeds smoothly. The structure and its attendant debris are truly cold and dead. Such battle-damage is familiar to your salvors, and this survey even easier due to the apparent lack of live munitions and none of the usual frozen or fried vacuum-desiccated remains.

Sensing a laxity in the crews, your ops chief growls admonitions over the general comms then orders a more detailed adherence to procedure.

Reports and scans come in, a detailed holographic mapping forms in your primary display, and all data confirms with certainty that this is an Onslaught Mk.I vambrace.

The modular armor features structural attachment points, flux vent ports, and even an auxiliary sensor network, though no internal power sources.

The engineering is all recognizable as related to Domain standards, but as if from a crude half-foreign culture. Power connectors, for instance, are robustly built but oddly shaped and accompanied by an odd drift in signage iconography. Your salvors have understood and adapted to these oddities before. Nonetheless, it is technology unlike even the oldest nanofabricated hulls and hardware designs used in the Persean Sector.
options: gsVambrace_salvors3b:"Tech, what are we looking at here?"

text:
"Judging by the battle damage..." Your technology officer looks back at the specs, struggling. "There was a battle."

A look of concern grows. "It has been here for a long time, but from the debris cloud... and just one vambrace, abandoned here? The conditions are strange enough, but I would speculate that this is not an untouched site. It doesn't look like salvagers or scrappers, they've got distinctive patterns. I couldn't say who, or what, but I suspect the site has been disturbed. Or moved."

"If only it had datacores, buffers, something to analyze!" Tech looks at your ops chief, who gives a single shake of their head.

id:
# take Vambrace sample to Tanaika 
# and/or give her a Oneslaught sample?

script:
SetOptionColor DEVoyaTanaica_askSample gray
SetOptionColor bffi_talkToEngineer0 gray
options:
50:DEVoyaTanaica_askSample:DEV talk to nanoforge engineer about samples
50:bffi_talkToEngineer0:DEV do BFFI_TALK_TO_ENGINEER convo (janky)
oyaTanaica_askSample:"I'd like you to look at a material sample. I'll pay."

text:
"Hmm," she gives you a renewed look of interest. "You're the one who had the glove. Right." 

"Well you had me at 'pay', captain. Come on down and we'll have a look at... what did you say you had?"

options: oyaTanaica_sampleVambrace:"A piece of modular armor found in the Abyss."

notes: # Maybe later.

text: She shrugs. "It's your credits."

"The Abyss?" Her eyes widen. "You're braver than you look. Or stupider."

"Anyway, come on down. I'll feed some excuse to traffic control."
options:
oyaTanaica_sampleShuttle:Shuttle down with the armor sample
oyaTanaica_sampleBack:"Never mind."

text:
"Alright."

She glances aside, not bothering to hide that she's checking the time.

Descending to Asher, you glance out the viewport of your shuttle.

Stars disappear from the black tapestry of space as it begins to glow with a deep violet-blue before sinking into dim, ruddy brown.

The thick clouds of suspended fines are whipped away by the shuttle's flight through cold winds, and the shadow is replaced by the generalized sandy murk of Asher.

* * *

"Doesn't look like much, does it," says Senior Engineer Tanaica as she takes the sample from you - a block of armor composite, edges rough from the salvors' plasma cutters.

She places the sample onto a shelf which retracts into the towering white casing of the analysis suite. Transparent panes reveal the machine's arcane inner workings.

"You found it in the Abyss, you said?"

A containment shield drops over the sample port with a satisfying clunk.
options: oyaTanaica_vambrace1:"A planetoid in the Abyss. Some nameless rock."

text:
"Most of 'em are, aren't they."

She manipulates the controls, evacuating the atmo from the sample container. Then she stops to look at you.

"If this is just a chunk of starship armor, I can tell you that for free. Saves you 10,000 credits."

"But if you want me to look real close and do some professional speculation," she holds out her datapad, ready to receive payment.
options:
oyaTanaica_vambrace2:Pay 10,000 credits
oyaTanaica_vambraceEarlyOut:"It's a piece of armor. I'll take it and go."

text:
She shrugs, and reverses the sample insertion procedure. 

Senior Engineer Tanaica sends you on your way back to orbit with no more farewell than a succinct "Right then."

Your TriPad pings confirmation, and hers responds cheerfully.

"Right then," she says, equally cheerful.

Tanaica's datapad disappears as she turns back to the controls. A loud humming grows - it feels almost ticklish in your teeth for a moment as she adjusts power levels.

Displays bloom with readouts, cheerful greens abounding. A holo of the sample flickers into being.

The machine's hum drops an octave, and loses some of its urgency.

"Huh," she says. She wears a look of blank unease.

"Weird."

Tanaica manipulates her controls and a small cutaway pulls out of the rough block of armor. It scales to fill the volume of the holo.

Another segmentation of scale iterates, then another as Tanaica brings the resolution to the nearly-atomic. Patterns of false colour flash highlighting pseudo-crystals, various regular - and irregular - structures, as well as flaws.

"It's the damnedest thing, isn't it," she says, attention focused on the display.
options:
oyaTanaica_vambrace5:"Is it?"
oyaTanaica_vambrace5a:"Definitely. Wow. Look at that. The... ah..."

text: She turns to look at you, raising an eyebrow.

"Sorry, cap," she says. "I should be thinking out loud, you paid good credits for this after all."

She licks her lips, then releases a breath.

"It's nanoforged. Obvious stuff, look at that drift," she taps something and a distortion in the material structure becomes exaggerated.

"Crude, relatively speaking. Modern forges won't do it like this. Hm. 'Modern' meaning anything from the last thousand cycles. Seems like you found a genuine museum piece. Not unheard of 'course. Scavengers pull in bits of old sporeships and Explorarium junk often enough. Saw a bit of cryosleeper scanned when I was in training. Fascinating stuff."

She taps not the controls, but the casing of the analysis suite. "So it's old, but that's not what's strange."
options:
oyaTanaica_vambrace6a:"You like drawing this out for maximum drama, don't you."
oyaTanaica_vambrace6b:"So what makes it strange?"

text:
"Ha!" She smiles, if uneasily. "Got me. It's boring as dirt here in Luddie-land, gotta make my own fun."

"Still, this really is interesting... There's no embedded fabrication-rights!"

She looks at you significantly, as if this means something important.
options: oyaTanaica_vambrace7:"So? A hacked pirate forge will strip out the FRM."

text:
"There are no embedded fabrication-rights!"

She looks at you as if this means something important, and you're supposed to respond.
options: oyaTanaica_vambrace7:"So? A hacked pirate forge will strip out the FRM."

text:
"Sure. And they've been churning out garbage for 200 cycles. But what we see here is that this was made by a very old, very large forge. Probably top of the line for the time. And your sample has been exposed to space for a long, long time. And no FRM."

"Haven't seen this before." She rubs her fingers together, thinking. "Either someone was running a starsystem polity-level illicit forge a couple thousand cycles ago and you found the results, or... hm, you found something really weird."

She turns to you. "Where'd you find this? Some rock in the Abyss?"
options: oyaTanaica_vambrace8:"Some rock. Why can't it have been legally forged with no FRM?"

text:
"That's... that'd be insane. The whole point of the Domain was to control this stuff! Well," she concedes to herself, "Maybe the Gate Network was point number one."

"But regulating the nanoforges would have been two. Maybe three. Definitely important. Everything is stamped and tracked, that way you know who made what, where it ends up, when to collect forge-taxes, and who to blame when things go wrong."

"It was..." She shakes her hands at the analysis suite in an odd gesture, emphasizing the size of the concept. "It was the basis for interstellar civilization itself!"

She takes a deep breath, looking self-conscious at her own outburst, then busies herself with flicking at some controls; the machine slows its hum to a dull whine and then a sudden unsettling absence of a background noise you weren't even conscious of hearing.

"Some rebel polity in the Domain-era I guess? Maybe they had some frontier-freedom notions before the Domain Armada re-stomped them into compliance. I'd pay good credits myself to learn how in Ludd's seven hells it ended up on some rock in the Abyss!"

She catches herself, then grins. "Uh, not that I wouldn't appreciate that answer for free if you happen to solve it."

options: oyaTanaica_vambrace11:"I'll consider it. If I find out more"

oyaTanaica_vambrace11:"I'll consider your request."
oyaTanaica_vambraceOneslaught1:"Have you heard of the Onslaught mk.I?"

text:
The sample emerges, and - more gingerly, now - Senior Engineer Tanaica pulls on some gloves and places it back in your sample container.

"You find some interesting stuff out there if you don't mind me saying so, captain."

She nods approvingly. "Thank you for the subsidy. I'll be sure to enjoy myself once I wrap up this miserable Asher contract."

"Oh sure," she says.

The sample emerges, and - more gingerly, now - Senior Engineer Tanaica pulls on some gloves and places it back in your sample container.

"Wait." She looks at the sample container. "Really?"

options: oyaTanaica_vambraceOneslaught3b:"Really. I have one in orbit."

text: "Wow." She hands the sample container back to you.
"Wow. Guess that explains why the newscasts have been so boring today. Luddics, right?" She hands the sample container back to you.

"You find some interesting stuff out there if you don't mind me saying so, captain."

She nods approvingly. "Thank you for the subsidy. I'll be sure to enjoy myself once I wrap up this miserable Asher contract."
Senior Engineer Tanaica sends you on your way back to orbit with no more farewell than a succinct "Right then."

id: # Stranded in deep space with no Transverse Jump

text:
It can't be denied any longer.

You're stuck in deep space near an obscure celestial object having foolishly jumped in before ensuring your $shipOrFleet could jump back out.

The bridge officers are very carefully avoiding an outright panic, assigning makework maintenance duties and shutting down speculation. Still, you can't help but notice questioning glances - spacers will follow almost any order, but this is a serious blunder. 

Your navigation officer appears to be having a heated intra-ship comms discussion with your engineering chief. You catch key phrases, "too dangerous", "theoretically impossible", "worked once".

Navigation, deeply embarrassed to be caught mid-argument, salutes and explains.

It appears that a senior technician on hyperdrive duty is insisting on speaking with you. They have some kind of crazy idea about getting back into hyperspace and have somehow convinced your chief engineer that it's viable.

Ancient and rad-scarred, the senior technician is senior indeed, likely having more space experience than any other crew or officer in your service. The old spacer produces a minimum viable salute. "$PlayerSirOrMadam. All respect, but you've done a real dirt-boot screw-up putting the $shipOrFleet out in a nothing-nowhere gravwell."

Your navigation officer inhales to begin a reprimand, but the spacer burns ahead, "Good news is I've seen it possible to jump direct to hyperspace. Risky, though," they say, looking down. "Risky."

"But," a crooked smile appears, "Better'n having to eat the officers, aye?"
options: strandedInDeepSpace2:"What is your proposal, senior technician?"

text:
The legendary smuggler captain who showed this trick to the senior tech made them promise never to tell a soul about it. But seeing as how she died some forty cycles previously, and given the current situation, the old spacer is willing to bend the spirit of the agreement slightly to get out of this mess.

A modification to the hyperdrive field software will allow you to perform a kind of Transverse Jump directly to hyperspace from normal space. It's also useful for jumping through sub-viable jump-points which are inaccessible when making hyperjumps within Domain-approved parameters. 

Nav looks aghast and Engineering is shaking their head as they review the proposed modifications on a beaten-up TriPad.
options:
strandedInDeepSpace3a:"Will it work?"
strandedInDeepSpace3b:"What are the risks?"
strandedInDeepSpace3c:"Make the modifications and prepare to jump."

text:
Your navigation officer sighs, looking distraught.

"Theoretically," they say, rubbing their eyes.

"It's extremely unorthodox. This... this inverted resonance stacking notion," they poke at an arcane diagram which rotates along axes somewhat north of four dimensions, arcs and lines jittering with non-euclidean enthusiasm through the mere three-dimensional space of the holo.

"It shouldn't work. The math appears to give the correct solution, but to get there..."

Your chief engineer stops shaking their head to answer.

"Well. Our field generators weren't designed for this, but it should fall - just - within failure limits. We'll have to go to combat agrav, stow everything and everyone."

A sigh, as if giving up, then, "If it doesn't work, we're all dead and it'll be so quick we won't even know it. If it works, it's going to hurt. Expect a significant hit to operational readiness. And we'll need repairs."

A thoughtful look, "Of course, assuming it works, with the telemetry to calibrate from..."

Your navigation officer looks stricken at the thought.
"$PlayerSirOrMadam," they all respond. The old spacer grins unevenly once more to your senior officers' frowns.

With the automated defenses cleared, your salvors are free to crack open the enormous zero-G vaults of the cache. Power is provided to dead conduits, fused circuits replaced with temporary fittings, and the great doors persuaded to loosen their mechanical restraints.

At first, it appears to be empty. A colossal tomb, never filled. Then probe-mounted radar starts to build a picture in your bridge display while suit lights pile their beams into the space, catching drifts of dust and gas set afloat by the re-opening. Spars gleam in the dark, revealing trifold radial symmetry.

"$PlayerSirOrMadam," your tech officer says, attention fixed not on the suit cams or bridge display but on technical specs scrolling through a display already overfull with diagrams and documents. "You have to see this."

script:
$player.gotWormholeCalibrationData = true

text:
It's old. Very old. But the queries sent by your salvors' interfacers are returning green. For the most part.

"It's a wormhole anchor," your tech officer explains, excitement palpable. "Loaded with calibration data, and fuel to power whatever it was going to do. This was prepped to deploy at the nearby, um, point of stability. The automation went into standby at some point, and... here it is."

The enormous zero-G vaults of the cache lie open, its depths black with portent like a colossal tomb.

The ancient wormhole anchor waits at its center, the flicker and caress of status queries returning green, mostly. It is still primed for use at the nearby stable location, but the shipment of transplutonics its automated systems were waiting for were never delivered.

id: # Ice giant which the Gate Hauler orbits

script:
ShowDefaultVisual
FireBest PrintHasSurveyDataTextIfSo
FireBest OtherPlanetInteractionText
FireAll PopulateOptions
FireBest PrintNearbySurveyHostilesTextIfSo
text:
Your $shipOrFleet approaches $entityName.

The ice giant spins below your $shipOrFleet, lit only by the barest light of the galaxy, warmed only by the internal pressure of gravity and the barest flicker of atomic decay. Unlikely to form naturally in interstellar space, this planet must have been thrown from its home system by some cataclysmic interaction in aeons past. Adrift, it is doomed to shrink and cool toward the interstellar medium over billions of cycles.

Your $shipOrFleet approaches the $entityName.

It is a Leviathan from out of time, at once familiar from its many and varied depictions in the secondary culture of the post-Collapse Persean Sector.

But to see it here in the proverbial flesh, it is the alien force of the Domain's power reified before you, looming like a blackened emperor slumped dead - but dreaming still - on a throne of stars.

You feel the weight of history pressing upon your chest, a tone like a distant ringing. You are frozen for an unconscious moment as if your mind was held in the gaze of a god; and in the next instant it is nothing but the buzz of power conduits and low thrum of agrav within the drive bubble, usually relegated to a filter of unperception by your subconscious.

You adjust your uniform and turn to your bridge officers.
options:
gh_explore:Order salvage teams to perform an assessment
defaultLeave:Partir

text:
Your $shipOrFleet approaches the $entityName.

Before you is a Leviathan from out of time, at once familiar from its many and varied depictions in the secondary culture of the post-Domain Persean Sector. You can feel the magisterial force upon you; your officers and crew are hushed, humbled and reverent, as if they have found themselves before the corpse of a thunderous and cruel god who once ruled the skies. To awaken it, to touch the divine, must fall somewhere between sacrilege and sanctification.
options:
gh_activateCheck:Have your chief engineer review a plan to reactivate the Hauler
defaultLeave:Partir

text:
It is no simple task to appraise a Gate Hauler, for the ship is truly massive.

Your officers, however, have the tools, ancient datafiles, and a trained and ready crew. Known variants of Gate Hauler design are pulled up on a shared display to match to the example which looms over your $shipOrFleet. Your operations chief oversees the deployment of salvors to key points in coordination with targeted sensor sweeps.

Meanwhile, your tech officer and chief engineer review the deep archive containing flight statistics on thousands of recorded Gate Hauler deployments in order to anticipate the results of in-transit damage and subsequent self-repair protocols.

Ops orders the salvors to prioritize a data connection, and it's soon online via ancient protocols. Walls of amber error codes glow in your tech officer's interface. 

You have part of an answer soon enough: The functioning of the Gate Hauler's enormous but primitive Alcubierre drive system was massively disrupted mid-transit. The ship's automation could not ascertain the cause of the discontinuity and so, to protect the literally astronomical value of its cargo, forced the ship into an automatic failsafe mode.

Most of the remaining fuel was dumped and the rest used to slow into an orbit of the most accessible nearby mass concentration: this lonely ice giant. The maneuver took the better part of a century.

Standard procedure would have the Domain send a well-supplied expedition of Gate engineers to reset the primary systems and send the Hauler back on its journey, albeit many hundreds of cycles behind schedule.

The ancient records note that Gate Haulers would carry a standard complement of automated survey, extraction, and refining drones in the case of lost-contact. The documents seem to imply the ability to procure new sources of fuel and materials effectively at vast distances from the fail-safe point, though at the scale implied, this process would be inefficient at superluminal speeds even via hyperspace - assuming use of baseline Domain frontier technology.

Your officers conclude that, typical to the Domain, the publically available descriptions seem to elide certain key technologies and capabilities.

In any event, your survey turned up none of this - aside from the undeployed Gate itself, of course, held tight by the many-limbed face of the great starship.

The ancient records note that Gate Haulers would carry a standard complement of automated survey, extraction, and refining drones in the case of lost-contact. The documents seem to imply the ability to procure new sources of fuel and materials effectively at vast distances from the fail-safe point, though at the scale implied, this process would be inefficient at superluminal speeds even via hyperspace - assuming use of baseline Domain frontier technology.

You can only conclude that this particular self-recovery effort ended with the drone expedition sent to the Limbo system, and it was to use obscure Explorarium wormhole technology to resupply the Gate Hauler so that it could continue its mission. 

In any event, your search has turned up no additional technological treasures - aside from the undeployed Gate itself, of course, held tight in the many-limbed face of the great starship.

The details accumulate, each senior officer manipulating the shared display as reports are given. Finally, your chief engineer clears the holo back to a slowly rotating false-lit overview of the entire vast starship. They suppress a brief smile as they clear their throat.

"$PlayerSirOrMadam." A pause for anticipation, a rare indulgence by your habitually practical chief engineer. "I have the pleasure to report that it is possible to re-activate the Gate Hauler. It could be targeted to a known system within the Persean Sector at your command."

It just needs transplutonics. A lot of transplutonics. Unlike familiar starship engines, the Gate Hauler doesn't use the standard Domain AM fuel package. Once a critical mass of transplutonics is achieved, the Gate Hauler will be able to operate for at least a thousand cycles with functionally unlimited range within the bounds of the Persean Sector.
options:
gh_activateCheck:"Tell me how to turn this thing back on. I want numbers, chief."
gh_activateRisk:"What are the risks of reactivation?"
defaultLeave:Partir

text:
"The Gate Haulers were built for reliability," your chief engineer explains. "Every fail state reverts to a lower-energy safe mode; the backups of backups have backups. Even intentionally sabotaging a Hauler would involve extreme effort."

Your tech officer taps at a datapad with a vexed look, but raises no objection.

A silence descends. There is no need to put it into words: the arrival of a Gate Hauler in the Persean Sector would be momentous.

Your chief engineer starts nodding and tapping at their TriPad, mumbling over technical figures.

An interactive display appears in your executive interface, and with a few manipulations, the chief has a materials schedule prepared for your review.

script:
$activated = true
$activating = true 1
GateHaulerCMD removeActivationCosts
GateHaulerCMD activate
text:
Your operations chief oversees the transfer of transplutonics. Streams of cargo drones feed the unimaginably huge bulk of the Hauler; it seems strange that so little can move so much so far.

With only a little coaxing, the Gate Hauler's automation churns into something like machine-awareness; sensors reports power surges throughout the hull as ancient conduits and machines self-test - some failing. Long-dormant self-repair drones begin to seethe like vermin.

And, slowly, your $shipOrFleet's various sensors feel the growing power of the machine. Magnetic fields shudder, neutrinos begin to spill forth in earnest from reactions deep in the mass of the beast; gravitic fluctuations from the warp-pylons flutter, and your own drive bubble compensates.

It will take about a day, engineering reports, and then the Gate Hauler will be ready to receive navigational input.

Your $shipOrFleet approaches $entityName.

Energy surges throughout the vast hull as ancient conduits and machines self-test - some failing. Long-dormant self-repair drones seethe like vermin.

Your sensors officer's console lights up with incoming reports. Magnetic fields shudder and stabilize through test patterns, a growing stream - a river, a torrent - of neutrinos spill from reactions deep in the mass of the beast. Your $shipOrFleet's drive bubble compensates for gravitic fluctuations as space-time is tested.

The Gate Hauler is still awakening.

Your $shipOrFleet approaches $entityName.

The titanic ship lays potent before you, ancient but powerful drive fields thrumming through the membrane of spacetime. You can almost feel it through the agrav's self-correction, the drone of a god-machine. Almost.

Once provided with a destination, the Gate Hauler will travel through normal space - a Gate cannot be moved through hyperspace. The Hauler will travel faster than lightspeed, thankfully, due to its venerable Alcubierre drive. Indeed, the Hauler will quickly accelerate to an apparent normal-space speed which your $shipOrFleet cannot match with its relatively tiny in-system drives. The journey will still take quite some time, due to travelling significantly slower than standard hyperspace permits.

After it has arrived at the target destination, you may give the order to deploy the Gate to an unoccupied stable location.

gh_selectDestination:Select a destination for the Gate Hauler
gh_deployGate:Initiate Gate deployment
defaultLeave:Partir

text:
Your $shipOrFleet approaches $entityName.

While in transit, what's left of the electronic brain - distributed, multiply redundant, well-shielded - is utterly focused on its goal. There is no realistic prospect of altering the course of the gargantuan ship and, indeed, when it is in its acceleration phase, of keeping up with it as it picks up speed in normal-space.

The Gate Hauler rests in space, a potent mass jealously cradling its legendary cargo. It awaits final confirmation to deploy the Gate.

As it happens, the go-code requests telemetry from the Gate Hauler's launch as a pass-key. After several tense seconds, the re-launch telemetry appears to be accepted as valid input, and executive access is granted to your command console.  

Functionality is limited: Gate deployment can only be targeted broadly at a star system or roughly equivalent celestial structure. The Hauler's programming allows for no fine control and will select its own stable location. The process is irreversible and the order can not be countermanded once given. After deployment, the Gate Hauler will automatically dismantle itself, presumably to become feedstock for the next glorious expansion of the Domain's frontier.

A single word blinks on your command interface. DEPLOY?

You give the command.

The Leviathan awakens from its slumber one last time to perform the purpose for which it was built an aeon ago. Astride history, you feel its flow shift inexorably beneath you.
Your $shipOrFleet approaches $entityName.

text:
"An abomination, a devil-$shipOrFleet from Hell itself is before us! We pray to Providence to pass this test which has been given unto our unworthy-"

The $post's words are for $hisOrHer crew, not you, and the link shudders into static. The last image you see are hands clasped in prayer.

"The blessing of Ludd be upon-" The $post's eyes go suddenly wide at a red flash from a secondary display.

"By the holy pneuma," $heOrShe gasps.

"What by blessed Ludd's holy right glove have you done to your $shipOrFleet? Is that the cursed work of Moloch my eyes do perceive in your $shipOrFleet?"

$HeOrShe looks over $hisOrHer shoulder, "Summon the subcurate technologia! For immediate consultation!"

The $post turns back to you. "You have but a moment to explain what my sensors suggest, captain."
options:
CGRpatrol_weirdMods2a:"Is it my... unique modifications you object to?"
CGRpatrol_weirdMods2b:"There seems to be some kind of misunderstanding."
CGRpatrol_weirdMods2c:"What exactly do your sensors suggest?"

text:
"Unique mod-" $HeOrShe sputters.

"It is devilry! You've joined human craft with abomination. It is an unholy thing!"  $HeOrShe makes some kind of hurry-up hand signal over $hisOrHer shoulder.

"You condemn your soul to the abyss. And you condemn yourself as mortal enemy to the Church and accursed in the eyes of God and all who hold faith." $HisOrHer expression is grim.

The $post turns and exchanges a few words with someone you can't see.

"Methinks there is not, $playerSirOrMadam, and you merely dissemble with a devil's tongue to gain advantage." $HeOrShe makes some kind of hurry-up hand signal over $hisOrHer shoulder.

"Judgement shall be pronounced ere long upon what my heart fears."

The $post turns and exchanges a few words with someone you can't see.

"Overt devilry. You've combined human craft with abomination." $HeOrShe makes some kind of hurry-up hand signal over $hisOrHer shoulder.

"By this unholy 'technology' you condemn your soul to the abyss. And you condemn yourself as mortal enemy to the Church, accursed in the eyes of God and all who hold faith!"

The $post turns and exchanges a few words with someone you can't see.

"You're certain?" $heOrShe asks. $HisOrHer face has gone pale, as if $heOrShe has been informed that $heOrShe has been condemned.

The briefest flash of emotions: pain, fear, outrage, sadness, acceptance. The eyes that turn back to the comm-link are that of an enemy across battle-lines.

options: CGRpatrol_weirdModsOpt3aSel:"I am commissioned by the Church!"
CGRpatrol_weirdModsOpt3bSel:"Hold your condemnation for a moment, for I share your Faith."
CGRpatrol_weirdModsOpt3cSel:"Let's discuss this before jumping to conclusions..."
CGRpatrol_weirdModsOpt3dSel:"Perhaps we come to an 'arrangement'? Just name a price..."

text: "You are no longer," $heOrShe says.
"I abjure your heresy ," $heOrShe says.
"Corruptor, mammonite!" $heOrShe spits.

text: You try to establish a comm link, but get only static.
"Acknowledging receipt of AIS code. Good to see you, $playerName."
"Hostile $shipOrFleet, be advised: we've already dispatched a distress call to a nearby patrol, and it should arrive shortly."

The enemy $personRank looks grim but defiant. "We will fight you to the last," $heOrShe says.
OR
"Damn, we're in a tight spot." $PersonRank $personName suddenly notices the line is open and shuts it off.
It appears the enemy $personRank doesn't seem to feel the need to talk to you, as your comm requests go unanswered.
"You have been scanned and identified. It's probably best for both our peace of mind if you keep your distance."

"You have been scanned and," $heOrShe blinks at $hisOrHer screen, then says almost - but not quite - under $hisOrHer breath, "$playerName. The butcher." 

$HeOrShe straightens $hisOrHer back, "It'd be best if you kept your distance. We, we are armed."
OR
As the comms resolve, $rank $personLastName looks at you with recognition and alarm.

"It's... you. The things you've done, I can't believe-" $heOrShe clears $hisOrHer throat uncomfortably, "What is it you want?"
"Ah, Captain $playerName." $HeOrShe looks uncomfortable. "Your reputation... precedes you. Let us conclude our business quickly then go our own ways."

"Acknowledging receipt of your codes, moving to a secure channel." A flicker of a phoenix icon and static, then: "Yes? All is well I trust? Glad to hear it, citizen. Over and out."
OR
"Your $shipOrFleet has been scanned and identified, everything is in order. Burn bright, citizen."
OR
"Scanned and identified. You're flying pretty, citizen. Clean jumps and high burn." $PersonRank $personName signs off with a flash of the gold phoenix.

"Hostile vessel, be advised, linecast to system patrol craft has already been dispatched, reinforcements are en route. You are advised to power down weapon systems and disengage. Over."
OR
"Belligerent contact! Be advised that firing upon a Hegemony vessel is considered an act of insurrection and you'll be hunted down!" The Hegemony $personRank is sweating. "When they're done with you, you'll be sold in black markets on worlds across the Sector, I swear it."
OR
"We give our lives for the restoration of the Domain and all humanity." $HeOrShe pauses for a moment as if to say something more, then cuts the comm.

The Hegemony $personRank looks grim but defiant. "We will fight you to the last," $heOrShe says.
OR
"We fight and we die for the Domain; for humanity." The comm-line cuts out.

"By authority of the High Hegemon Administrator, you are ordered to take hyperspace drives offline, disable weapons and surrender. Over."
OR
"Resistance to Hegemony authority is an act of insurrection and shall be punished accordingly. Surrender now or be met with force."

"You have been scanned and identified, citizen. If nothing is amiss, we shall be on our way."
OR
The Hegemony $personRank looks impatient. "Is there an emergency to report? No? Then carry on, citizen."

"Identity confirmed. Please accept my greetings on behalf of the Company."
OR
"Sorry friend, this Tri-Tachyon vessel is on a classified mission. Burn bright."
OR
"Greetings on behalf of the Tri-Tachyon Corporation! Please state your business so that we may assist in meeting your needs -- for a reasonable fee, of course."
OR
"Comm open. Oh, do you have the latest Tri-Tachyon catalogue of executive spaceflight accessories? I'll have a customer service representative upload a copy immediately."

Your comms officer's console pings.
OR
"Hi, I'd like to add you to my professional network on TriLink."

"Attention. Your vessel has been scanned and identified as hostile to the Tri-Tachyon Corporation. Further attempts at aggression will be recorded."
OR
"Any attempt to hinder or damage this Tri-Tachyon owned and operated vessel will result in legal consequences." The Tri-Tachyon $personRank looks nervous. "Trust me, these people don't mess around, you really ought to leave me alone."

The Tri-Tachyon $personRank looks grim but defiant. "Corporate regulation 313.33, section A, states that combat-capable vessels must defend themselves in a vigorous manner at all times," $heOrShe says.

"Attention. Your vessel has been scanned and identified as hostile to the Tri-Tachyon Corporation. Prepare for annihilation."
OR
"On behalf of the Tri-Tachyon Corporation I would like to thank you for providing this opportunity to prove the superiority of our branded line of space defense products."
OR
"On behalf of the Tri-Tachyon Corporation it is my duty to inform you that this engagement will be recorded. As a presumed belligerent engaged in destruction of wholly owned Company property, you automatically refuse all rights to distribution and profit-sharing." The Tri-Tachyon $personRank grins and cuts the comm-link.

"On behalf of the Tri-Tachyon corporation, it is my pleasure to inform you that your $shipOrFleet has been scanned and its tactical loadout evaluated."

The $post drones in a pleasant corporate monotone. "Have you considered purchasing a Tri-Tachyon upgrade package? Visit the nearest Tri-Tachyon shipyard to find out more." 

"Please maintain your distance... and have a nice day."
OR
"Greetings on behalf of the Tri-Tachyon corporation!" The $post produces a cheerful, if hollow, smile.

"Feel free to admire our ships which are equipped with the latest in Tri-Tachyon space defense products and software, but please maintain a safe distance. The Company takes property damage very seriously."

"On behalf of Tri-Tachyon, it is my pleasure to..."

The $post halts $hisOrHer cheerfully plastic greeting. 

"Captain." $HisOrHer eyes dart to another display, "... $playerLastName. Please accept my greetings on behalf of the Tri-Tachyon corporation. I..."
options:
TTpatrol_weirdMods1a:"Thank you. Is everything alright?"
TTpatrol_weirdMods1b:"Is there some kind of problem, $post?"
TTpatrol_weirdMods1c:"Is this about a bribe? Just tell me how much."

text: "Yes, quite alright," $heOrShe says. "It's just I've never..."
"No, not at all. Nothing to worry about. I think. I mean, no, but..."
"A bribe? Ah, no, not at all. Nothing like that, captain. It's- well, you see..."

$HeOrShe pauses, then begins again.

"Your $shipOrFleet features some unusual modifications." $HeOrShe glances at a secondary display. "Very unusual."

$HisOrHer eyes dart back to stare through the comm-link.
options:
TTpatrol_weirdMods3a:"Is that right? How interesting."
TTpatrol_weirdMods3b:"Do you like them?"
TTpatrol_weirdMods3c:"Is there going to be some kind of trouble?"

text:
$HeOrShe pauses, then begins again.

"You appear to be operating a very unusual ship, captain." $HeOrShe looks, wide-eyed in disbelief, at a secondary display.

$HisOrHer eyes dart back to stare through the comm-link.
options:
TTpatrol_weirdMods3OMKI:"Is this about the Onslaught Mk.I?"
TTpatrol_weirdMods3a:"Is that right? How interesting."
TTpatrol_weirdMods3c:"Is there going to be some kind of trouble?"

text: "Yes. And... yes." $HeOrShe clears $hisOrHer throat.

A look of deep consternation crosses the $post's face. The answer is clearly 'no', but $heOrShe seems unable to say so. After several agonized seconds, $heOrShe manages to produce a response.

"I've... never seen anything like it."
"I don't... no, no trouble." $HeOrShe clears $hisOrHer throat.
"...Yes," $heOrShe says. "Yes, I suppose it is."

"There's a message I am supposed to deliver." $HeOrShe looks at $hisOrHer secondary display.

"Tri-Tachyon's Director of Special Acquisitions, Zunya Glamor-Rotanev, would like to invite you to a meeting. On Eochu Bres."

$HeOrShe looks back to you. "'At your earliest convenience', it says. And something about a reward."

options: TTpatrol_weirdMods5b:"Tell me, what does 'special acquisitions' entail?"

text:
$HeOrShe looks aside again. "My apologies, I wasn't meant to tell you that part. The reward is for me."

A smile breaks through $hisOrHer nervous demeanor. "-For finding you. I should thank you, Captain $playerLastName."

"So... Thank you."

$HeOrShe looks skeptical. "You don't... You haven't heard of Director Glamor-Rotanev? Hmm."

The $post takes a conflicted moment to think.

"Let's just say it's above my pay grade."
