text:
$HeOrShe looks at you for a long moment, a touch of amusement hiding sadness.

"I suppose if you're not from around here, you'd have to have it told."

"Used to be, Mairaath was a wonder of the Persean Sector. Three great cities in orbit, the Astropoli, and below a wonder of terraforming biotechnology." Bitterness enters $hisOrHer voice now, "Some Pathers took offense to people living long and well and without fear of the dark, so... "

"They blew it up," $heOrShe says, stone-faced. "The mad fanatics, they blew it up. Most terrible act of terrorism the Sector has ever had inflicted, if you ask me. Tens of millions dead, all the dreams and world turned to ash. The real miracle is anyone survived." $HeOrShe indicates $himOrHerself, "My ancestors did, somehow."

"Mayasuran until the end. And beyond the end, because not even the end of our world could kill us all off. Might be living off the scraps, but we're still living."

$HeOrShe is proud, still, despite Mairaath's lost glory.
"Contact. Comms open," says $Rank $name. "We are ever-watchful in defense of holy Gilead."

$HeOrShe blinks. "Why, the Prophet Ludd was called to Gilead, in the Persean Sector, in a divine vision. Ludd came and touched the earth of Gilead and declared it a gift of providence, and thereupon called all the faithful to turn it into a garden world, a living example of the sacred balance of life, a place of pilgrimage."

"Believer or no," $heOrShe says earnestly, "you would be welcomed to visit the shrine on Gilead and see for yourself."
"Reading you clear, super-green all 'round," says $Post $name. "Don't worry, we're just doing honest business. Not touching a thing on Gilead, no $playerSirOrMadam!"

options: greetingsAskTouchGilead:"Wait, why can't you touch Gilead?"

text:
"Oh now, you don't start too," $heOrShe says. "I didn't mean anything by it." 

A long pause, then $heOrShe says, "Ah, you were asking for real. Well, it's the Luddic holy world, isn't it? They're very strict about who goes there and what they get up to. You don't want to mess around with the Knights of Ludd, they couldn't tell a joke from a declaration of war."

"Keep your nose clean around them and their world, that's all I'm saying." $HeOrShe nods, "Free bit of advice."

"Sometimes I ponder what my life would be in the Domain - an uncollapsed Domain, that is," $heOrShe says, contemplative. "A glittering sea of living worlds, each filled with history."

"Though I suspect someone who went needling around asking uncomfortable questions might get into a spot of trouble, eh?" $HeOrShe gives an unacademic snort. "I've managed to produce enough of that - trouble, I mean - for myself in the here-and-now. If you don't anger the institutions you're not doing good work, I think."

"Oh 'Infernium', yes yes. It's a bit of Luddic vernacular, refers to antimatter starship fuel. Though what they really mean," and here $heOrShe stresses the word, "is rather more."

"In certain texts the term encompasses all AM applications, or even all high-energy processes. 'The get of Mammon and Belial', one creative firebrand called it. I like that one; I've always enjoyed the dramatic rhetoric of Luddic folk-preaching tradition."

"Now I may be old, but there are older by far. If the records are to be believed - and I do believe, the Hegemony keeps very good records - then the pirate Warlord Kanta was born well before the Collapse. Or shall we call her ex-Domain Armada officer Jorien Kanta?" $HeOrShe looses a wheezing laugh. 

"Oh yes, she had a name like anyone else, once upon a time. One wonders if it's expensive biomods or periodic cryosleep that keeps her going. I'd love to find out..."

"Hmm, history is not all the study of 'great leaders', but one can't help but admire a narrative of glorious victory, hubris, and ignominious end. Philip Andrada, for instance."

$HisOrHer gaze drifts into the past as $heOrShe speaks, "I remember like it was yesterday. He embodied all that was proud and good in the Hegemony. Practically deified on every holo following his defeat of the Warlord Loke. Oh, how we celebrated at that news! It was the vanquishing of chaos itself, the dawn of a new golden age. So it felt, once."

"His turn wounded us," $heOrShe says softly. "It wounded the Hegemony itself, very deeply." $HeOrShe nods to $himOrHerself, drifting back into some old memory.

"I've considered cryosleep. Pay the fee, jump ahead a hundred cycles to see how everything turns out. As a historian, by the stars, it's quite an appeal! On the other hand - yes, yes - I know the statistics. The failure rates are low nowadays... if you find a good vault. But it still scares me. You could just go to sleep and never wake up." 

$HeOrShe laughs darkly, "Though that's every single day, isn't it? Ah, I have too much work to do, anyway..."

"Aliens! Hah. No, there's nothing well documented enough to be considered," $heOrShe stresses the words, "Legitimate History."

"Yes yes, if you want to experience xenolife, visit Jangala and take your pills and quarantine shower."

"But that's simply not what is meant, is it. We want there to be someone else out there like us, someone to talk to, someone to tell us we're not alone, someone to give us answers. Someone who cares. Perhaps we're looking in the wrong place? Or thinking in the wrong way?" $HeOrShe dismisses the thought with the shake of $hisOrHer head. "Eh, but this grows into speculation. Not my trade!"

"We've all heard the stories about the Onslaught battleship, the final weapon that stood against an alien threat." $HeOrShe scoffs, waving a dismissive hand, "As if we live in some milfic holo about brave heroes and vile aliens."

"Grand historical context-breaking contact doesn't work that way, at least in our body of knowledge. It's more confusing; messy and chaotic. It undermines the entire cognitive-ideological basis of society, which is then forced to reform around a great wounding of its very epistemological basis. And always, so many must die to learn these lessons."

$HeOrShe gets a distant look for a moment, then breaks into a sardonic smile. "Not unlike the Collapse eh? Must be aliens." $HeOrShe winks. "For sure."

"Old Earth, mother Terra. From whence we came, long gone. The Luddics aren't completely wrong in their stories. Though, hm, they possess few qualms about 'improving' details to appeal to their quotidian morale framing."

"The historical record is far more complex than that telling, and far more difficult. Far more relatable in the end as well, I believe. Every day we survive by powerful technologies which any one of us barely understands, and which may unleash truly apocalyptic destruction. Mistakes are made, tragedy results; it's only a question of scale."

$HeOrShe considers this for a moment, then begins mumbling a critical response to $hisOrHer own thoughts, "But scale can indeed fundamentally change the quality of phenomena and hence the contextualization of the moral dimension, hmm, quite entirely! Hmm..."

"Though much of the Persean Sector has been effectively freed from the Domain's technology restrictions - without dismissing the Hegemony's best bloody-minded efforts, of course," $heOrShe gives a half shrug to their efficacy, "-this age has not seen any especially great leaps of technological development. Why not? For the Persean League, or Tri-Tachyon among others, the ban is lifted!"

"Perhaps it is that development of highly complex, advanced technologies requires a stable foundation: a large population base with excess theoretical and engineering capacity, a willingness to devote resources to pursuits beyond mere survival. Or war. And yes, war drives innovation - of a sort. But imagine the possibilities if those billions of credits had not been vaporized alongside so many foolish youths... like those I once knew."

$HeOrShe goes very quiet for a moment. "Those old production chips and nanoforges offer a ladder with the middle rungs missing. We fight to reach toward the top, doomed to fall as the step falters..."

"The Human Domain split key industries into separate worlds, although yes, with redundancies - set well apart from one another. If one world which produces hyperdrives tried to leverage their specialization against the Domain, well, what good are they without raw materials? Or AM fuel? Or, indeed, the basic necessities of life and access to markets?"

"How can one rebel when the Domain need only control the local Gate to lay a complete siege? Which one world could possibly stand against the entire Domain?" $HeOrShe gives a grim smirk. "Now we're stuck here, besieged by the Domain. There's no one to accept our surrender but some dead Gates."

"The Galatia Academy? Oh yes, yes, I once attended. And taught there too, if you'll believe it. For quite a few cycles. They figured me as a troublemaker soon enough, but I was slippery. I played the game. Me! Hah, would you believe that?"

$HeOrShe laughs at some old memory. "Made a few enemies too, I did."

"Did you hear who they put in charge of the Academy? Anahita Baird! Can you believe it? The wolf is tending the sheep now, and that's the truth. The Hegemony has no idea what they've done." $HeOrShe cackles, "No idea!"

"With Baikal Daud in the office of High Hegemon, I can't help but wonder at what mark his administration will make on the 'Phoenix of the Persean Sector'. The old guard still dominate the navy - that's the Eventide contingent. Those families go back to the arrival of the 14th, of course. It's not an old aristocracy by any historical standard, but the pattern is established quickly enough given agreeable conditions. Where was I- yes, Daud. Man of the people. Ruffles feathers. Like when he intervened personally in the Sphinx ensigns scandal. Hegemons simply don't do that! But he's a born leader, none can deny it."

"That's what is really going to get him in trouble," $heOrShe says, then goes quiet, lost in thought.

"In my circles- that's historians, mind you, not just old folks hanging around the bar," $heOrShe chuckles at $himOrHerself. "We've made a rule not to argue about Artemisia Sun unless legitimate new information appears. And it never does!" $HeOrShe pauses for a moment, tapping $hisOrHer book. "But you're not a historian, so the rule doesn't apply. Wonderful, you get to hear me out!"

"So: Why was she voted off the board just in time for the crucial military disaster of the Second AI War, then able to sweep back into her office with the credibility of all who opposed her absolutely annihilated? Was it merely a ruthless recovery, or was this a calculated sacrifice of Tri-Tachyon assets in order to consolidate her personal power from the start?" $HeOrShe smiles. "Maybe we can hash this theory all the way through one of these days."

"How we long for some incidental recording of even one of Ludd's supposed speeches or acts. Incredible, is it not, how all evidence of a prophet seems to have disappeared? This, hrm, 'miracle' is of course a point of faith among followers of the Church of Galactic Redemption." 

$HeOrShe raises an eyebrow at you. "It is also, officially, a point of policy for the Hegemony to cooperate with this nonsense in order to appease those Faithful." $HeOrShe furrows $hisOrHer brow in frustration, "As a historian, of course, this vexes me to no end. History as experienced by the common person may be a story society tells itself, and distorted as such. Even still! It must be grounded in fact."

"Of course if I did discover a holovid of Ludd's Trial, some fanatic would try to murder me soon enough for distributing it. Not that I could help myself." $HeOrShe smiles impishly, then considers. "If you do ever find something like that in some old derelict, you'd give me a copy, wouldn't you?"

"You wouldn't believe how much of my work is built upon cargo manifests. I have a former student working on Agreus; they send me copies lifted off scrapped memory cores. The real secrets are wiped, of course, but the story is all there, in the lines of onloaded and offloaded cargo compared against the hull mass calculations used by navigation to say nothing of the hyperdrive. History is written in logistics!"

"Take for instance the sheer quantity of food and supplies that 'disappear' on trade routes skimming the edge of the Core Worlds. Statistical sampling suggests that a significant percent of all economic activity in the Persean Sector is 'off the books', and a significant portion of that goes straight toward those who are called 'pirates' as well as the so-called 'decivilized' populations... to say nothing of undocumented populations within the Core itself."

The historian leans close, "The official population census of the Persean Sector could be off by as much as an order of magnitude. It's simply astounding." $HeOrShe smiles, and shaking $hisOrHer head.

"Ah, like I was just telling those young folks over there," the historian waves $hisOrHer hand toward a small group which seems to be avoiding eye contact, "- after the trials of the Cold Passage, the 14th Battlegroup limped across the Persean frontier to discover a sector dominated by the Warlord Leonis. The joint command of the 14th struck down that cruel reign and were hailed as liberators, laying the groundwork for Admiral Kali Molina to make a declaration of Hegemony over the Domain colonies of the Persean Sector with the Eventide Diktat of cycle 49."

"The untarnished Hegemony of that time was too overshadowed by memories of the Domain to truly appreciate the political capital at the High Hegemon's command. Short-term thinking prevailed in anticipation of Gate reactivation. Indeed," $heOrShe puts on a look of sad understanding, "How could they have known that discontinuity would persist for so long, perhaps forever?"

"Whoever Ludd was, they were not a mere uneducated rabble-rouser. Besides," $heOrShe lowers $hisOrHer voice, "I subscribe to the theory - held by more than a few secular scholars, though quietly, quietly indeed - that Ludd was not one, but many. I would argue that the present format of the Holy Ecumene Council of Archcurates is a reflection of the early Luddic leadership network passed down through history, institutionalized in the early years post-Collapse."

"Heresy, of course. But the truth generally is," $heOrShe says with a smirk.

"The contemporary form of the Church of Galactic Redemption has proven remarkably stable in spite of, or perhaps because of, the chaotic political arrangement of the Persean Sector immediately post-Collapse."

The historian cradles $hisOrHer hands, unconsciously repeating the old motions of lecturing.

"The Holy Ecumene Council of Archcurates rule on matters of canon, authorize and delegate executive functions, and perform certain exclusive sacraments. However they may be censured and even laicized by a supermajority of the Curates-popular! The members holding those more common positions are ratified by local congregations but must be ordained by an Archcurate in good standing."

$HeOrShe sips some tea, then frowns and looks at $hisOrHer cup as if the tea had gone unexpectedly cold. "Of course, it's rather more complicated than just that in practice..."

"There are those among the Church of Galactic Redemption - the Luddics, in popular parlance - who hold that the Knights of Ludd are an aberration at odds with the original Luddic vision of the Church. Doubtless true," the historian raises $hisOrHer eyebrows at this small heresy. "An examination of what are agreed by scholars to be among the more authentic passages of the Book of Ludd suggest that themes of militancy and 'a knighthood of the faith' are likely intended as abstract references to a sort of semi-sacral militant volunteerism which would range in practice from a civic protest movement all the way to, arguably, insurrectionism."

"The practical benefit of a standing militant order during the Post-Collapse chaos and following wars must be admitted, however," $heOrShe leans back in $hisOrHer seat, contemplative. "And the most vocal objectors within the Church proper argue that the Knights err by degree rather than in whole. The less vocal objectors? Well, we may not know their thoughts for many, many cycles indeed. The Knights see to that."

"It is speculated that Admiral Andrada, the self-anointed Supreme Executor of the Sindrian Diktat - or 'Sindrian Mutiny' per the Hegemony viewpoint - is facing a dictator's all-too-common problem of succession. Until now, behind the unified facade of the Diktat, it appears that he has played his major supporters off one another to maintain political stability. Declaring a clear successor would have disrupted this internal balance." 

"And now," the historian speaks slowly, rubbing $hisOrHer chin, "with the cycles' inevitable turn and Andrada in rumored poor health, will he make a surprise announcement to designate an heir? There would be a wave of arrests and, dare I say, executions as those loyal and disloyal to the Executor-apparent are... sorted."

"Or perhaps Andrada has a secret family, or a mind-simulation, or a clone, or some wilder scheme?" $HeOrShe barks a laugh. "Speculation on this point seems to say as much about the speculator as about anything Andrada is thinking. I should ask you for some insight!" $HeOrShe laughs again.

"If Philip Andrada, the self-anointed Supreme Executor of the Sindrian Diktat, were the strategic mastermind he was claimed to be - first by Hegemony propaganda and then his own - then why then did he not simply sweep the Persean Sector in a conquest to rival his greatest vanquished foe, the Warlord Loke?"

"Well!" The historian raises a finger like an orator, then, thinking better of it, lets $hisOrHer hand drop to the table. "Matters abound! Andrada took a portion - but only a portion - of the Hegemony navy in the course of his mutiny. He took possession of the Askonia system and was immediately faced with the catastrophic consequences of the Opis incident, ongoing rebellion, complete lack of logistics, and the pressing need to maintain a credible military force against a vengeful Hegemony. And, indeed, all of this while fulfilling the expectations of key supporters!" 

"The true testament to Andrada's genius may well lie in his scheme not immediately collapsing in upon itself!"

"The culture of transient interstellar starship crew and infrastructure workers - "spacers", if you like - has become particularized in the era post-Collapse. Rather than existing in vast diffusion across the Human Domain as a mere statistical category, impossibly detached and undistinguished, they've been localized to the Persean Sector and now form a critical mass of intra-cultural contact out of which distinct commonalities arise."

$HeOrShe takes a moment to catch $hisOrHer breath before proceeding. "Indeed, the majority of interstellar logistics have shifted from Gate to hyperspace journeys. Spacers bear everyday witness now to the ruins of the Collapse and AI Wars. These journeys are haunted," $heOrShe takes on a jovially grim tone. "Haunted, one might say, by history. Indeed, one might suggest that Spacer culture has become quite superstitious in the last two hundred cycles."

$HeOrShe trails off, letting a breath go, looking upward toward unseen stars. "The great dark filled with death and mystery as never before," $heOrShe says, quoting something.

"Old Mairaath, the center of the old Mayasuran stellar polity, was primed to blossom as a shining beacon of culture and civilization in the Persean Sector, a light to outshine Opis perhaps, once the terraforming came into true fruition."

"Both those lights are extinguished, now," the historian says gloomily. "I suppose they lacked the hard-hearted military-industrial alignment of the Hegemony's Eventide-Chicomoztoc axis, to say nothing of Kazeron and its League. And so on, for the rest of them."

"The worst of us thrive in these violent times, it seems." $HeOrShe seems to realize in the moment who $heOrShe is speaking to and adds, without conviction, "Uh, present company excluded, of course."

"What precisely the Hegemony does with the 'forbidden' AI confiscated by its technological inspectors, particularly during the post-AI Wars crackdowns, is not - yet - a matter of historical record. And unlikely to ever be, if COMSEC has their way," $heOrShe says bitterly. 

"Obviously," $heOrShe continues, "the Hegemony is more conservative in exploitation of AI relative to the Domain at its height. The grumblings of the Luddic population ring loud in any Hegemon's ears, and Baikal Daud knows better than to spark unrest under the present peace-of-compromise."

"Now that said," the historian leans closer, lowering $hisOrHer voice, "The paranoid provide entertaining speculations. One theory tells of secret overminds hidden in far-off planetoids simulating worlds which hold in thrall the minds of billions of bio-suspended human slaves; cruel scenarios playing out in the sick imagination of uncaring AI-gods. Nonsense, of course," $heOrShe smiles reassuringly. "What purpose would such mistreatment serve?"

"Those who awake from cryosleep who were laid to rest in the time of the Domain are perhaps the most lost of all. Many, though not all, imagined they would populate the far end of a rich frontier; they accepted that they would leave behind all they once knew. Now they all find themselves cut loose from all reference, unmoored in history."

"And who is to care for them? Certainly the Luddics suggest the comfort of their religion. Others take up drink or drugs, or nihilistic detachment which too-often spills outward into destruction. Not a few of the most brutal pirates are former sleepers, you know."

"Where records persist, sleepers who are uniquely skilled are sometimes... recruited, drafted - 'kidnapped' is perhaps the word - by parties seeking insight into some old tech or lost secrets." A troubled look crosses $hisOrHer face. "I admit that if I could, I would arrange for the systematic interview of the sleepers. They would prove an excellent resource to our pre-Collapse histories, provided we keep a realistic understanding of the limitations of first-hand sources."

"I find myself with a growing affinity for the developing itinerant spacer culture," $heOrShe gestures to the patrons around you. "They have developed a colorful milieu, worldview, and even a shared mythology."

"Although," $heOrShe leans in, "on a personal level, I cannot but wonder if something of the world itself has changed since the Collapse. Has hyperspace become more chaotic, mysterious; inhabited, almost, by alien phenomena? Or was it simply unobserved, or unreported before? Or were the reports suppressed? Or was there simply not enough intra-spacer contact to reinforce their observations - or shall we put it, for the skeptical -  these shared narrative projections?"

"I am a mere historian and, now," $heOrShe smiles sadly, "I am bereft of the corrective presence of my colleagues in the Academy. I both fear and admit that my documentation efforts might appear to drift toward dilettantism."

"The mind reels at the loss of the Collapse," $heOrShe says. "Set aside even the Domain for a moment, with its population inconceivable to our barely-civilized human brains. Just the fringe world of the Persean Sector witnessed untold death as the thin thread of logistics from the Core, and the Domain itself, was cut. Food, life support, medicines, essential machinery - bled out, spilled into space to become so much debris..."

The historian stares off into a corner, $hisOrHer eyes darkened, $hisOrHer attention become lost in the imagined scene of past horror.

"Ah, the tragedy of Opis. The sheer scale of loss is incomprehensible to the mind. To this day there are those who fight and kill over the finer points of whose fault it was. I have my position, but that argument over-dominates the story of the Opisean polity itself. Why should those countless dead be defined by their murderer? I find that the beginning of a story is necessary to illuminate the end, don't you agree?"

$HeOrShe doesn't wait for an answer, but begins to lecture. "Opis was a center of population, of art and commerce. Not heavy industry as-such, however - that they exported to Sindria; and agriculture, to Volturn. Out of sight, out of mind. The rulers of the Chartery of Opis grew to see little need in appeasing any but their local patronage networks. Wealth flowed to the center, where influence coagulated; the edges were forgotten."

"Was it once again the sin of the Domain re-enacted on a smaller stage?" $HeOrShe raises $hisOrHer eyebrows.

"This?" $HeOrShe pats the paper book $heOrShe keeps close, and consults often. "It is... something of an affectation fitting with my self-image."

"There are independent high-tech manufacturers of such objects which range far from traditional papermaking. The integrated digital interface paper-analogs are impressively functional, but my own notebooks? I purchase them from a Luddic craft guild on Tartessus. Indeed, it is mostly composed of dead cellulose."

"I do produce digital backups," $heOrShe admits. "It is not in keeping with full faith in the spirit of the material, but the archivist and researcher in me cannot abide the risk of losing my notes."

text:
A patron does a double-take, and with recognition comes a look of intense hatred.

"You!" The shout cuts through the clatter and noise of the bar like a shot. The burly spacer stands, and readies a meaty fist...

Only to be thrown back onto a table with a smash of glassware by one of your bodyguards - generally discreet, but paid well to take initiative at times like these.

The chaos begins to spread outward, crew and locals alike staring, and those drunkest and angriest standing.

You hear your name spoken like a curse, and a makeshift missile shatters on the support column behind you.

Your bodyguards form a human wall to cover your path toward the exit. Stumbling outside, you can hear the alarms which signal the rapid approach of local MuniSec. There'll be no quiet business done in this bar for the shift, that much is clear.

The chaos spreads outward, crew and locals alike staring, and those drunkest and angriest standing. One pulls a knife, which prompts a pirate three tables down to draw a gun, at which point your instinct to find cover kicks in.

You hear your name shouted like a curse, and an already broken vidscreen on the support column behind you explodes into burning plastic fragments, the soft-round from a boarding pistol putting it well out of its misery.

Your bodyguards respond with controlled bursts from energy weapons set to low-power, giving cover to your withdrawal. Smoke, screams, and flashes of discharge fill the bar. Stumbling outside, you see passers-by clearing out as fast as they can. A noodle stand cook gives you a frightened look as they duck behind armored shutters. There'll be no quiet business done in this bar for the shift, that much is clear.

The chaos spreads outward, crew and locals alike staring, and those drunkest - clearly, this bar doesn't only serve tea and small-beer - and angriest standing.

You hear your name called like a curse, and a makeshift missile smashes against a samovar behind you. It tips, slowly, and falls to the ground with a crash. A small wave of hot water washes over your boots, and a cloud of steam envelopes you.

Your bodyguards form a human wall to cover your path toward the exit. Stumbling outside, you can hear the bells which signal the rapid approach of Path anti-vice enforcers. There'll be no quiet business done in this bar for the rest of the shift, that much is clear.

Your bodyguards form a human wall to cover your withdrawal. Stumbling outside, you hear the buzz of riot control drones which signals the rapid approach of gendarmerie. There'll be no quiet business done in this bar for the rest of the shift, that much is clear.

The chaos begins to spread outward, crew and locals alike staring, and those drunkest and angriest standing. A couple executives in the back roll their eyes and activate a privacy field as their bodyguards step out of the shadows to protect their small pocket of peace.

You hear your name shouted like a curse, and a makeshift missile shatters on the support column behind you, cracking a vidpanel into fractal nonsense.

Your bodyguards form a human shield to cover your path to the exit. Stumbling into the corridor outside, you can hear alarms and the buzz of surveillance drones which signal the rapid approach of one or another of the corpo security franchises. There'll be no quiet business done in this bar for the shift, that much is clear.

The chaos spreads outward, crew and locals alike staring, and those drunkest - clearly, this bar doesn't only serve tea and small-beer - and angriest standing.

You hear your name called like a curse, and a makeshift missile smashes against a samovar behind you. It tips, slowly, and falls to the ground with a crash. A small wave of hot water washes over your boots, and a cloud of steam envelopes you.

Your bodyguards form a human wall to cover your path toward the exit. Stumbling outside, you can hear the ringing of alarm bells which signal the rapid approach of the local MuniSec squad led by a Knight of Ludd. There'll be no quiet business done in this bar for the rest of the shift, that much is clear.

text:
The clink of teacups is suddenly overcome by a shout and a crash.

"Demon! Servant of Moloch, follower of Mammon!" A disheveled figure with a scraggly beard leaps on a table, leveling a pistol toward you, "I walk the Path! I strike at you with the Hammer of-"

A series of dull cracks signals your guards' suppressed low-penetration fire and the assassin falls like a ragdoll.

The samovar behind him, indented by a through-shot, leaks a steady patter of hot water into a growing pool of blood on the table.

Local MuniSec holds you, your guards, and a few witnesses until a mid-rank Knight of Ludd can make a ruling on the situation. The case for self-defense is clear-cut - witnesses swear holy oaths to this - and the licensing of your protective weapons is accepted.

"A child, led astray," the Knight says gruffly as the would-be assassin is wrapped in a simple shroud and taken away. Their eyes turn to you, "Your pride sows wrath, and it bears this bloody fruit."

After a pointed silence, they say, "You and yours; go now. What's here is done."

"...So I pray," the Knight mutters as you turn away.

$HeOrShe glances at a secondary display, checking your $shipOrFleet ID.

"Charity may be a virtue, spacer," $heOrShe says, "but I'd only even consider your request if you were actually in need - and commissioned in service of the Church of Galactic Redemption."

The $post gives you a strange look, then glances at a secondary display. Then, looking slightly alarmed, back at you.

Something comes up, $hisOrHer eyes narrow, expression growing harder. More tapping. Then $heOrShe turns to you, frowning.

"You don't become the $post without having seen a few tricks over the cycles. I just didn't expect a captain bearing a $faction commission to attempt the old cargo dump on me."

$HeOrShe sighs, shaking his or her head. "I'm flagging your commission ID for a cycle. Now get off my comms."

"I'm a $post for the Hegemony, not some two-credit League world. I've seen the old dump-the-cargo done by an upstart who thinks they're more clever than the system before, and I will again. You should be hauled before a court martial. But lucky you, I've got enough bureaucratic make-work, so I'm going to pretend this was one big, stupid mistake."

$HeOrShe sighs, shaking his or her head. "I'm flagging your commission ID for a cycle. Now get off my comms."

"I see your trick, captain, and while I admire your drive to maximize profit, I cannot respect that you're too incompetent to dump your supplies further afield before requesting assistance. You understand that if I was found misappropriating Tri-Tachyon property, they'd bond me into debt-service? I've got wealth, but not much. It'd be life."

$HeOrShe sighs, shaking his or her head. "I'm flagging your commission ID for a cycle. Now get off my comms."

"I may walk with Ludd, but you don't become the $post by taking every spacer's word on faith alone. I'd hoped a captain commissioned by the Church would never attempt a cargo dump scam on me, but here you are."

$HeOrShe sighs, shaking his or her head. "I'm flagging your commission for a cycle, that you might reflect and pray for forgiveness. Now remove your sinning face from my comms."

"I'm no gens-get, captain; I earned my position by serving from Laicaille to Salamanca and I didn't become $post because I'm a fool. I just didn't expect a League captain to attempt the old smuggler's dump on me."

$HeOrShe sighs, shaking his or her head. "I'm flagging your commission ID for a cycle. Now get off my comms."

"You don't become the $post here without knowing a few tricks employed by terrorists and smugglers. I'd have expected if you'd pledged to the Sindrian Diktat you'd know better than to attempt the old cargo dump scam. Stars beyond, you'd get us both sent to Cruor!"

$HeOrShe sighs, shaking his or her head. "I'm flagging your commission ID for a cycle. You'd best hope the Spider doesn't take an interest. Now get off my comms."

"Hmm. Your $shipOrFleet is in decent shape, but your readiness has suffered. Seeing as how you bear a $faction commission, it looks like I might be able to justify providing you with assistance."

$HeOrShe gives you a severe look. "Are you sure you want to call this in now? A commissioned captain is only authorized to receive emergency supplies once per cycle."

"Your $shipOrFleet has taken some serious damage. You have a $faction commission and this is a clear case of need, so I shall provide you with emergency supplies."

text:
You make the arrangements, and before the passing of a workshift, a small batch of emergency supplies is transferred to your $shipOrFleet for repairs.

"The funds you receive from $theFaction are meant to cover operational expenses," $personName says brusquely. "Your commission ID will be flagged for one full cycle due to this emergency supply transfer, so don't make a habit of not having a backup plan."

Your salvors spread out through the $nameInText in teams under the gruff direction of your Ops Chief. Salvage is rapidly identified, assessed, logged, and tagged with trackers, when appropriate, for follow-up teams to extract.

Computers and datastores are accessed via disposable interfacers, rough and quick hardware jobs that send back any information they can lift from poorly shielded databanks. Back on your $shipOrFleet, your tech teams crack security and decrypts anything they can, forwarding useful information - maps, cargo manifests, engineering diagrams - back to the salvors to use.

These teams are experts at rapidly reconstructing the relevant events of a starship or space station's demise; such a narrative provides useful information about dangers and valuables.

Here, comms logs and surveillance gives meaning to the evidence of scattered damage caused by light firearms - local security was in the process of a major crackdown on a drug smuggling operation, and in an explosive confrontation appears to have caused a blowout of the primary hab section. This seems to have tipped the state of the station, already precarious, into a full evacuation.

The rest of the story is mere salvage for your waiting holds.

Your salvors fan out through the station, discovering makeshift barricades around the access tubes to the executive and security modules. There's interior combat damage caused by handheld weapons, many of which appear to have been adapted from mining tools. A strike turned deadly.

The security forces employed riot-suppression armaments at first, then rapidly escalated to their cache of "vac-safe" weapons intended for boarding and infantry action within ship hulls or station modules. 

The miners appear to have responded by up-armoring standard haz suits, adding breastplates worked from mining drones' blast panels, slotted visors cut from rolls of nuke-rated anti-spall sheeting, and much other imaginative makeshift panoply.

With the tides turning against them once more, security forces fought a break-out action toward central life support.

Successful, they choked off atmo and water to those modules held by the strikers. This won the fight, if the heavily armored but nonetheless desiccated corpses floating through the corridors have anything to say about it, but the war was still lost.

The station appears to have been abandoned as-is, too much of a scandal to reactivate or, perhaps, merely a write-off.

Your salvors discover that comms and anti-collision weapon emplacements have been deliberately destroyed by careful strikes.

Closer examination shows evidence that a pirate ship docked - the brutal hull markings and garish paint job of the gutted hulk are a giveaway - but was completely disabled in the cargo bay by some kind of makeshift explosive adapted from on-hand mining equipment.

Their plundering mothership destroyed, the remaining pirates - operating a handful of light frigates and a wing of fighters - appear to have taken out their anger by strafing the station's hab modules and life support before going on their way.

The few miners who survived the immediate attack were doomed to a slow death.

Your salvors proceed with a routine ingress, by-the-book under the suspicious eyes of your ops chief.

An atmo sample of a trapped pocket - a tedious checklist task - pull up bright orange and green on the shared display: extreme biohazard. The salvor working the derelict almost drops the sampler-drill in surprise; Ops barks sharp orders, reiterating the hazardous environment recovery ingress checklist with effortless recall.
It's an unusual reading, but not dangerous if containment procedures are followed to the letter.

It's an unusual reading, and you recall the harsh words Ops gave you after returning to your $shipOrFleet after you exposed yourself to the xenolife of Jangala, albeit wearing a mask.

You also remember...
options:
salSRBW_opsRespA:Interrupting the tirade to inform them that you are the sole executive authority on this $shipOrFleet.
salSRBW_opsRespB:Thanking them for emphasizing procedure, but to be mindful of their tone in front of crew.
salSRBW_opsRespC:Making a crack about 'containment procedures' and visiting spacer bars which made the crew laugh.

text:
It's an unusual reading, and you'll never forget the dressing-down Ops gave you after returning to your $shipOrFleet after you exposed yourself to the xenolife of Jangala without even the benefit of a mask.

You also remember...
options:
salSRBW_opsRespA:Interrupting the tirade to inform them that you are the sole executive authority on this $shipOrFleet.
salSRBW_opsRespB:Thanking them for emphasizing procedure, but to be mindful of their tone in front of crew.
salSRBW_opsRespC:Making a crack about 'containment procedures' and visiting spacer bars which made the crew laugh.

text: Your ops chief also remembers, it seems, for they meet your eye just briefly as they give orders for the savlor teams to adjust dispositions in response to this discovery.
Your ops chief also remembers, it seems, for they meet your eye just briefly as they give orders for the savlor teams to adjust dispositions in response to this discovery.

Your ops chief also remembers, it seems, for they meet your eye just briefly as they give orders for the savlor teams to adjust dispositions in response to this discovery. As you recall, Ops didn't even smile. They never joke about procedure.

Never.

One particular module of this research station appears to have been a bioweapons laboratory. The lab is - rather, was - hermetically sealed, and contains the ruins of state-of-the-art containment and decontamination facilities.

Naturally, something went terribly wrong. 

And as your techies analyze telemetry and what raw surveillance buffers the salvors on-station are able to transmit back to your $shipOrFleet, the disaster begins to look distinctly unnatural.

"Sabotage," your ops chief mutters darkly.

The patchy surveillance shows a rapid spread of violent, painful demise beginning in atmospheric processing and spreading outward at the speed of fresh air through atmo conduits, tripping the broad-spectrum biohazard sensors one by one as the agent suffused through the station. But no alarm sounded for human ears.

Your salvage chief orders all remaining pockets of atmo purged, and strict quarantine procedures followed.

The bioweapons lab's computer core destroyed itself with a small bomb when a timed dead man's switch was not reset, so nothing of salable value remains, if indeed a buyer could be found for whatever biochemical horror was conjured here.

Salvor teams cut through fused locks and infiltrate the station. They find researchers, administrators, technicians down to the lowliest access-tunnel scrubber at their work-stations, or near enough. Quite dead, however. And even those who remained in pockets of viable atmo are surprisingly undecayed.

Their end was not pleasant, but it appears to have at least been very, very quick.

Your salvors and tech teams assemble evidence to reconstruct the event: a massive radiation pulse centered on Experiment Module B, which, judging from the gap between the ruins of Modules A and C, was almost entirely vaporized by the event.
options: salSRRS_story1:"Any sign of what they were trying to do?"

text:
Your tech officer shakes their head, showing characteristic frustration at having their curiosity unfulfilled.

No relevant monitoring and computer system from Module B survived, and the general practice of this station appears to have involved heavy siloing of information even up to the level of the executive command section, so it's impossible to say what exactly the research being pursued involved or, indeed, whether the event was an accident or something more sinister.

Whatever it was, at least it happened a long time ago.

Although superficially intact, salvor teams soon find evidence of light weapons fire and the resulting carnage, thankfully long desiccated, decayed, and/or frozen depending on the run-down time of life support in any particular sector. 

The seemingly random spread and directionlessness of the violence suggests that the security systems of this station turned on its occupants all at once. The catastrophic results were not helped by the unusually strong internal security measures: hidden autoturrets set to kill, powered lockdown curtains which indiscriminately scissored obstructions, and nests of mobile suppression drones which could traverse small conduits and air ducts to find and "neutralize" any survivors.

Thankfully, the last of these measures has long run out of power.

Despite the station's reactor being run down, and the power network itself highly degraded, your salvors are understandably paranoid. Your Ops Chief is more than satisfied to use their fear to emphasize the necessity of strict and systematic recovery protocols in this - and indeed every - recovery operation.

Try as they might to piece something together from station logs, it remains unclear to your tech team what precisely used the chief administrator's executive access codes to turn the security systems inward.

A precise decapitation strike appears to have targeted the command and control module of this station, likely a clutch of military-grade torpedoes unleashed at close range by an unsuspected vessel.

Security systems were tight and highly hierarchical; once the central C&C went down, automatic lockdown procedures trapped the occupants of the station in their sections - habitation, labs, research, recreation, life support, cargo handling, storage, engineering, cooling, and so on.

Now each is a locked tomb preserving evidence of the final hours, sometimes days, of the occupants.
Your salvor teams do not tarry to ponder the fate of these unfortunate souls, each miserable in its own way, as they cut through the lockdown doors to assess valuables.

Upon entry, your salvors discover first one, then many of the same portrait adorning the interior sections of the hab. Smashed busts line the primary reception concourse. Above all stood a statue of the selfsame likeness churned out of a simple rock-foam fabricator, polished, and gilded; it now lies face-planted into the deckplates, the gilding half-stripped.

All bear the face of a strong-jawed man who looks down upon the empty halls and dead plazas of the hab, his gaze fatherly; stern, and proud. And in all cases defaced: the nose smashed off, crude words painted or, more often, burnt across his image.

Even his name, whatever it was, has been erased from every databank by a spitefully determined noise-wipe virus.

Exploring further, the salvor teams discover the remains of a palace dominating an entire section of the hab.

Once a vast construction, now just two blackened support legs remain amidst the rubble of its shattered visage. Parklands, now reduced to lone and level ash, follow the curve of the habitat's shell.

Whoever the man was, he was first exalted and then hated in equal measure.

A close approach by salvor teams shows a heat source, and glint of artificial light. An anomalous pocket of operational life-support, the emergency subsection backup of a backup somehow running for tens, hundreds of cycles? It is unlikely.

Your officers always perform the standard transponder pings at the start of a salvage operation; it is part of the ritual of legal salvage (though records of legality are fabricated easily enough, when required, and doing a good job of it is a sign of respect appreciated by customs officers across the Persean Sector).

It would be awkward to stumble into some hermit's cell, ideologue's oubliette, or pirate holdfast unannounced, so your comms officer takes charge of a new signalling attempt with a full range of communication protocols, from an overpowered standard holo-link to mere radio, laser staccato, and even an esoteric grav-pulse relay.

"No, $playerSirOrMadam," Comms replies. "Nothing. With your permission, I've got an idea..."

An acoustic inducer-transponder, normally used in mining surveys, is tenderly attached to the hab's structure by a discreet remote probe. It thuds out a code, and detects only a tiny, noisy, rhythmic vibration in return. Something tapping on the hullplate with slow, clock-like regularity.

It doesn't change.

Your Ops chief oversees a careful breach of the habitat, wary both for traps and the need to not disrupt the surviving bubble of life-support. Survivors make salvage rights more complex, but they're generally thankful enough for rescue to come to a profitable accommodation.

Salvors attach temporary bubble-locks around the entry points, inflate them with the ubiquitous portable atmo units, and perform a cold-cut on sealed emergency doors. Slow and safe. Ops nods, satisfied with the work proceeding on the shared display.

A spot of green-yellow brightness at the end of a foggy corridor; the image stabilizer kicks in, cancelling the jerky hop-step of the lead salvor's micro-g movement.

A tiny garden grows wild within this celestial tomb. Some kind of fissile backup battery has survived, clicking away the decay of heavy elements to heat and light a tiny bubble of life. Moisture condenses in a murky lens of slush on a hubward window, then falls slowly in huge drops through the micro-g of the habitat's spin, past the glowplates, toward the mosses and molds and tiny flowers growing on the warm heaps of droppings and husks of vermin.

On a life-giving terran world, this would be nothing more than a dank pit. Here, it is a miracle.

text: The vessel, heavily battered and with a minimal power signature, drifts through space. The silhouette is instantly recognizable but somehow crudely foreign.
The ancient war machine drifts, it's hull battered and scarred by aeons-old war. Power signs are minimal

Your sensors officer begins a preliminary high-power scan of the dark mass as your operations chief preps salvor teams.

"$PlayerSirOrMadam, this looks... there's nothing quite like this on file, but..." Sensors gives up on words and pull a detailed false-color scan into the primary display.

Someone lets out a gasp. 

The database assistant algorithm won't provide a positive ID, but that form is unmistakable to the human eye.

This is the Onslaught Mk.I, the ship of legend.

How old must it be, thousands of cycles?

If you had been asked a shift ago, your reasonable reply would have been to call it a myth. The Onslaught Mk.I could easily be a just-so founding narrative element of the Human Domain, a story about united struggle and hardship against an implacable Other with a shiny bit of military kit in center stage. An imagined avatar of the empire's holy trinity: military, industry, gate system.

Yet here that avatar appears, unbelievably far and wayward from the heart of the Human Domain - or even what was once the Domain's frontiers a thousand cycles ago.

Your ops chief, unperturbed, looks to you for confirmation. Words are unneeded, the question is clear: send in the salvors?

Ops nods, then turns to bark commands on the salvors' command frequency.

Junior officers scurry to cajole crew prepping cargo-handling drones while Sensors puts one analysis team on careful outward watch and another on the hulk to ensure there are no surprises.   

Within short minutes, the first salvors dock their open-vacuum shuttle frames and utility drones at various entry points identified along the vessel's massive hull.

Ops gives the go-order.

script:
ShowImageVisual salvor_explore_hull
AddTextSmall "T H R E A T  D E T E C T E D" bad
text:
You notice your comms officer furiously working their interface, then turn and call a report to you.

"$PlayerSirOrMadam! Salvor-four is picking up something on an open frequency- um, radio-only. Very low power. Some kind of analogue encoding? We did this in... training..."

A few more taps, and a message is formed from noise, letter by letter, across your display.

script: AddTextSmall "T H R E A T  D E T E C T E D" bad
text:
This gives your officers pause - Ops calls a hold order while the situation is assessed. Internal self-defense systems activating? War-drone deployment?

Only silence.

Then acoustics picks up a few taps as salvors secure themselves to bulkheads, powering down their tools and, no doubt, reaching for what small-arms they do carry.

Still, no movement.

Low, steady EM activity - just the repeater, a chorus of them in fact, mechanically intoning the same message to one another.

Fevered analysis proceeds. In the midst of what appears to be structured noise buried in the analog waveform - datasets which are insensible without context - analysis teams discover a set of coordinates; a position at some distance from the small gravity well you're orbiting in archaic but parseable Domain astrogation units. The message still repeats itself.

"The signal is weak," your sensors officer says. "Unlikely to be picked up by anything that isn't in close-order formation with the... the hull. Tactical telemetry to coordinate distributed systems?"

Cautiously, team by team, your operations chief reactivates exploration and assessment of the hulk.

Bafflingly crude automation is wormed throughout the ship, whispering to itself. Interfaces are built on familiar fundamental principles, but aesthetically red-shifted, seeming from an alien culture.

What records remain are damaged where they aren't missing entirely. The logs tell of aeons of war in the darkness between the stars. Human crew in and out of cryo, continually diminished, replaced by the augmented wounded, then the cyborg rebuilds, and then, and then... this exhausted humming, clicking thing threaded throughout the ship.

The logs are empty for long periods- dark, hibernating leaps. Then there is a noted uptick in activity less than 200 standard cycles ago. The enemy is only identified as "Threat". A datastore reference leads to blocks of information decayed by time. But the reconstruction algorithms leap into action and, despite troublingly low reliability ratings, sew together a set of files. 

Patch notes.

The hardware specs are arcane, the software obtuse and plagued by null library calls, but the common language of human mathematics sketches out the intent. There, your technology officer's team assembles a new spec of the sensor modifications required to detect this "Threat".

Adapting these requirements on your relatively modern systems is a far easier task than the labors described by the ancients.

text:
A man appears. The resolution is poor, the audio patchy. It appears that he didn't pay for premium-grade transmission and caching.

"Ahem. Yes, hello? I assume that icon means it's recording."

He clears his throat, turning to fully face the imager.

Academician Cornelius Elek appears before you. The resolution is poor, the audio patchy. It appears that he didn't pay for premium-grade transmission and caching.

"Ahem. Yes, hello? I assume that icon means it's recording."

He clears his throat, turning to fully face the imager.

"I am Academician Cornelius Elek." He waits a moment to allow his name to sink in.

"You may have heard of my seminal work, 'A New Survey of Planetology on the Fringe of the Persean Sector' which achieved a new standard for the field, if I may say so, despite tepid reactions from certain of my narrow-minded colleagues. Now, as I was saying..."

"I am Academician Cornelius Elek." He waits a moment to allow his name to sink in.

"You may have heard of my seminal work, 'A New Survey of Planetology on the Fringe of the Persean Sector' which achieved a new standard for the field, if I may say so, despite tepid reactions from certain of my narrow-minded colleagues. Now, as I was saying..."

His self-important speech is as droning as you recall from your previous conversation, when you returned a planetology datacore to the Academy.

So this is Cornelius Elek. 

You recall discussing him with Alviss Sebestyen when you returned a planetology datacore to the Galatia Academy for a reward. Sebestyen wouldn't say it outright, but it seems that Elek is a man who tends toward a certain amount of interpersonal friction.

Judging by the self-important delivery of this speech, this is that same Elek.

He pauses a moment, probably to evoke gravitas.

"It is my desire that this missive finds you receptive, Captain..."

Elek glances down, reading from a datapad.

"$PlayerName. It has been strongly emphasized to me by... a patron of the sciences who has seen fit to fund my work that you are the perfect facilitator to the solution of an old mystery I found in the archives. I-"

He squints again at something on his datapad, then says under his breath "Is that the cost per second? Stars above!"

text:
"Of course, $playerSirOrMadam," the agent says. "If you'll come right this way."

You are led through the station's primary transport hub and are soon deposited in an airy plaza halfway around the ring of the station. Your junior agent leads you through crowds of students and past a few tight-lipped academicians who scurry by, clutching datapads.

The junior agent fills the air with the sort of patter they must have saved for first-cycle students, pointing out landmarks and remarking upon departments and figures of interest to niche fields of study.

You arrive in a quiet hall off the main concourse, only a couple decks above the station rim. An open volume ringed by railings provides a dizzying view of the clouds of Pontus 'below' you.

"Third door on the right, $playerSirOrMadam." The junior agent makes a tight smile then disappears.

A man in academician's robes is buried in a holodisplay. Several moons appear to orbit his head.

"What have I told you hooligans about office hours?" he shouts out. "They don't start until-" 

He finally looks and realizes you aren't one of his students. 

"Ah." He adopts a look of stern prognostication, cradling his hands together as glowing orbs circle above. "I suppose greetings are in order. I am Academician Cornelius Elek, senior vice-chair of the Planetology Department of the Galatia Academy."

"And you are?"

options: gaDHO_visitElekIntro2:"I'm $playerName. We've spoken before. I returned your datacore."
gaDHO_visitElekIntro2:"I'm $playerName. You invited me to see you."

text:

"I'm afraid I don't recall," he says. "What, may I ask, is the purpose of your visit?"
options:
gaDHO_visitElekIntro3:"You sent a message about doing a job."
gaDHO_visitElekIntro3c:"You sent a message about 'an old mystery'. I'd like to help solve it."
gaDHO_visitElekIntro3b:"You sent me a message. I was interested in the 'vast fortune'."

text:
"Oh yes, of course. You should have simply said so."

He rushes out of his holodisplay to pick up a datapad, then places himself behind a large desk, waving you toward a chair. Unfortunately it is occupied by a battered datacore, the sort used in long-range probes.

"Now then," he taps at his datapad, failing to notice your obstacle.

"The mystery concerns a very old dataset which has been in the Galatia Academy archives for somewhat more than two hundred cycles. I stumbled upon it in the course of my studies - well, in fact, I was discussing the old archives with... a friend, a wealthy patron of the sciences. A comment they made reminded me of its existence."

"As it happens, this dataset is a recording of telemetry from a Domain-era sensor array out on what we now call the Fringe. It was somewhat more civilized back when this data was captured, though not perhaps by much."

Elek chuckles softly at his wit.

"As for the dataset, we only really have a complete sourcing header. Our copy is mostly corrupted due to anomalous  degradation during hyperwave transmission and then over 200 cycles of low-priority storage."

He shakes his head with deep disappointment at the mistakes made by someone else.

text: He either doesn't notice or doesn't acknowledge your signal.

"So it does," he says. "Please don't touch it."

"In any event," Elek cradles his hands, looking into an imagined middle-distance. "This patron and, ah, friend offered to fund a project to recover an intact copy of the data."

"From the source." He smiles, quite pleased with himself. "An expedition shall be made to the original sensor array! Your name came up, captain."

"By revealing this to you, captain, I am trusting in your discretion," he says with a solemn nod.

"An excellent question." Elek taps at his datapad, and turns toward his holodisplay.

The spinning moons have disappeared to be replaced by a map of the Persean Sector, a broad swath of stars. The Core Worlds are highlighted, the fringe stars somewhat dimmed.

"Here you see - or do not see, for it is in the crudest sense a sort of absence - the Orion-Persean Abyss. It is far from a nothingness, however. And it is that not-nothingness with which we are concerned."

text:
"Well, $playerSirOrMadam, nothing about the rock itself."

"The Domain Explorarium ordered a non-routine survey of the volume. We don't know what they were looking for, but the survey program found... well, it appears as though something happened, something violent. A series of high-energy events over a short period of time a little over 200 cycles ago. A starless rock is, um, very unlikely to produce such effects naturally."

They pause, then speak tentatively. "A battle, maybe? We can't say for sure. I don't know what would be worth fighting over out there. Or even looking for."

There's only one way to find out.

And it's best done with plenty of extra supplies and fuel. Every spacer knows that the Abyss is not to be trifled with. Of those foolish captains who attempt to find their fortune there, the few who return are often starving, having to mothball ships for fuel and supplies during the long crawl back to the Core Worlds.

"$PlayerSirOrMadam," your sensors officer says. "There's a, a large ship in orbit. I mean no presumption, but I think you're going to want to get a closer look. The long-range readings are... unusual."

script:
ShowDefaultVisual
PrintDescription 1
AddText "A routine scan shows that this sensor array has been modified."

text:
Your sensors officer casts the visual output to the primary bridge display. It is all struts and masses and harsh geometric shadows - then with a flash, a LIDAR overlay reveals the structure in the light of a false day.

A section near the center flashes at Sensor's prompting. There- a small workframe has been attached to the central processing module, a clutch of equipment cases spewing wires and cables outward like a parasitic growth.

Your ops chief deploys salvors with caution bordering on paranoia despite all appearances of this arrangement being of standard civilian parts. If it's pirates seeking a detection advantage, it would be customary for the arrangement to be trapped, and your ops chief's custom to oversee their disarming with seeming ease.

Each check is made, each movement coordinated with your sensors officer to ensure there are no surprises. The salvor teams look for everything from crude chemical bombs set off by millimeter-thin thermal detector patches to invisible thresholds of exotic physics which surround certain advanced technologies that might have been plundered from some old Domain cache.

Everything goes quiet for a moment. Ops taps a console, then turns to you.

"$PlayerSirOrMadam. It's clean."

Ops orders a start to the real work now. A senior salvage technician boots up a small, cheap interfacer which they hold out on a simple telescoping pole to probe the mod-hardware control system. Telemetry pours into the isolated computing environment set up by your technology officer's team, and they begin to interpret the results.

"Well, we think it's from the Galatia Academy," Tech says, eyes on an expanding visual model of the computing environment.

"Um. It's written on the equipment cases. See, here." They pull a zoom-panel up on their TriPad. "It says 'property of the Galatia Academy Planetology Department by the authority of Academician Cornelius Elek..." their words drift off as the tech team's finding begin to coalesce into comprehensible patterns.
 
The story comes together: this old sensor array scanned a broad swath of the Abyss for very particular signatures over 200 cycles ago. Someone installed this new hardware quite recently, and it extracted that data from the sensor array's buffers, processed it using a clever suite of algorithms concocted as, presumably, the thesis project of some bright student of the Galatia Academy.

Then no one ever came back to collect the data.

gaDHO_visitElekAltNoPaymentLie:"I will forgo payment. Advancement of knowledge is wealth enough." (lie)

text: He taps his datapad. "There. With that bit of unpleasantness out of the way, we can focus on what is truly important- the data."

"That's sheer piracy! I'll offer 75,000 and not a credit more, $playerSirOrMadam," he says, growing heated.

"This petty haggling is beneath the station of a respected academician such as myself."
options:
gaDHO_visitElekAltPayment75k:"I accept."
gaDHO_visitElekAltIntroPiracy:"100k or I walk."

text: He almost strikes his datapad as he enters the transfer command - with a deep frown and judgmental look for you.

text:
His eyes widen. "Truly? That is to say, marvellous! I did not take you for a champion of science, but you may yet force me to re-evaluate my first impression."

Elek smiles, and seems to mean it.

options: gaDHO_visitElekAltWhatIsIt:"So, what does the data mean?"

text:
"Well." He pauses to make a thoughtful expression.

"That's a complex question to answer. The data," he manipulates his datapad, pulling up charts and diagrams in his large holodisplay, "It provides the opportunity for many interpretations, often contradictory..."

He proceeds like this with little sign of stopping. It's soon clear to you that he doesn't know.
options: gaDHO_visitElekAltWrapup1:"My people say it's pointing at a rock in the Abyss. Something happened there."

text:
"-Yes, of course." You see a flash of annoyance as his lecture is interrupted, but Elek manages to adjust to a new line of discourse.

"A rogue planet, a singular celestial body bereft of a stellar primary - or any other, for that matter. Cold, dark, and alone; bereft of parent star, flung into the sparse wastes between the arms of our galaxy."

"There are many such bodies although, unfortunately, we only rarely have the opportunity to study them closely these days..."

He begins speaking at length on the study of rogue planets.
options: gaDHO_visitElekAltWrapup2:"If you don't know what, then why? Why this array data? Why these coordinates?"

text:
"I didn't say I- it's a, presumably, unexplored-" Elek is caught off-balance again. "The point is..."

He sighs, surrendering a small battle.

"It was... my friend, the patron of the sciences. Given their, ah, extreme wealth and interest in acting as a sponsor of pure discovery, they had the notion to fund an expedition to solve my little mystery. I thought it was a capital notion! All was arranged. Then, of course... and you found... well." 

He goes quiet, finally.
options:
gaDHO_visitElekAltWrapup3:"Who is your patron? If they're so wealthy, I'm sure I've heard of them."
gaDHO_visitElekAltWrapup3z:"Are you sure your patron is only interested in pure discovery?"

text:
"Most certainly!" he sputters, "She assured me, at length, and despite her position in Tri-"

His mouth shuts and he looks intently at you. Hoping that you didn't hear the obvious.
options:
gaDHO_visitElekAltWrapupTT2a:"I shall say nothing, of course."
gaDHO_visitElekAltWrapupTT2b:"Tri-Tachyon is backing your project?"

text:
"Mm," he frowns, carefully recomposing an expression of lofty erudition.

"They have asked me not to reveal their identity. I believe, if anything, the humility they show in not taking public credit for their contributions to this great academy - and my department in particular - are a sign of dedication to pure scientific advancement. Truly, it is a noble undertaking."
options: gaDHO_visitElekAltWrapup4a:"Are you sure you can't tell me?"

text: "Alas." He shakes his head, "It would be unthinkable."

"Mm," he frowns, carefully maintaining a thoughtful expression.

"They have asked me not to reveal their identity. I believe, if anything, the humility they show in not taking public credit for their contributions to this great academy - and my department in particular - are a sign of dedication to pure scientific advancement. Truly, it is a noble undertaking."

He lets only a moment pass. "Not unlike your own refusal to accept payment - a brave, unexpected act."
options: gaDHO_visitElekAltWrapUp0:"I feel they are a kindred spirit. Indeed, are not we three all?"

text:
He chuckles softly to himself, looking pleased. "I'm not sure the Director would quite agree, she is..." Elek looks upward, as if to the stars, putting forward a thoughtful expression. "She is a woman of great vision, far greater than I would have expected from a lofty position in such an ostensibly profit-driven enterprise."

"By revealing what I have to you, captain, I am trusting in your discretion," he says with a solemn nod. "I dare not say more, for Tri-"

His mouth shuts and he looks intently at you. Hoping that you didn't hear the obvious.
options:
gaDHO_visitElekAltWrapupTT2a:"I shall say nothing, of course."
gaDHO_visitElekAltWrapupTT2b:"Tri-Tachyon is backing your project?"

text: "Good. That's... very good of you, captain." An extra wrinkle remains on his forehead.

"I, um. Don't be absurd! I could have been starting to say any number of words."

He doesn't look confident.
options:
gaDHO_visitElekAltWrapupTT2c:"I shall say nothing, of course." (lie)
gaDHO_visitElekAltWrapupTT2a:"I shall say nothing, of course."

text: "Indeed," he gives a tight smile, not taking the bait.

"Regardless," he sweeps his arm, "You have the opportunity of a lifetime before you - with your capabilities, you can travel to those coordinates deep in the Abyss and discover its secrets."

"Just... bring me back a record of what you find." Elek's eyes are fixed on you now, greedily.

"Recordings, telemetry, deep scans. A series of high-energy events happened there over 200 cycles ago, and these sort of phenomena simply don't occur on their own on these lone planetoids."

His gaze wanders off toward abstraction.

"Spontaneous eruption of a hyperspace dark-matter agglomeration upon interaction with a gravity well at sufficient velocity? A simple collision between celestial bodies? It would be an astoundingly rare event - one in a billion cycles..."

Academician Elek begins to lose himself in hypothetical scenarios, mapping them into his holodisplay and comparing simulated interactions with the dataset you provided.

His vision is bound to the walls of his ivory torus - while yours may soon look upon that which lies at the heart of this mystery, in the darkness beyond the dark.
You're not sure Elek even noticed you leave.

id:
# gaDHO talk to Academician Cornelius Elek during the mission
NOT $global.gaDHO_foundArrayFirst

text: "Yes? Aren't you going to the sensor array? I don't see how there can be any confusion about what I asked you to do."

"Ah, greetings $playerName!" Elek looks the happiest you've ever seen him.

"I am most eager to learn of your discoveries in the Abyss- perhaps you could summarize them to me while your mission data is transferred." He taps at a datapad, "I've opened a stream."

He awaits your reply, smiling.
options: gaDHO_elekGoToLocation2:"I haven't actually gone to the rogue planet yet."

text:
"What?" He's still smiling, but his joy is fading. "Why not? Did you need to pick up more fuel and supplies? Surely the Galatia system doesn't have the best prices..."

The smile has faded by now.

"Regardless, there's no need to check in with me. Go, get moving! I await your results with the utmost anticipation. And, in the meantime, I'm a very busy man. Good day."

He turns as he cuts the comms.

options: 100:devAdonya_visOption2:DEV make Adonya visible in comm directory

text: Done

It takes a few minutes for the comms answer.

Adonya appears, flustered. "Y-yes, hello? Who is this on comms?"

You can hear the babble of holo-entertainment over the link.

options: adonya_aboutScylla1:"I'm calling about your daughter, Scylla."

text:
"Oh, you're the officer from Fleet, aren't you," she says, misidentifying you.

"Did you find Scylla? You Fleet people haven't told me anything useful at all. It makes me wonder what my taxes are going toward, to be perfectly honest."

options: adonya_moreHolos:"Comms more often? She's been in contact? But you said..."
adonya_importantToFind:"I don't think I've emphasized how important it is for me to find Scylla and Elissa."
adonya_importantToFind2:"It's extremely important. The fate of the Persean Sector is at stake!"
adonya_notFleet:"I'm not from Fleet, ma'am. I run an independent operation."
adonya_tell:"I found Scylla on Laicaille Habitat, then..." (explain)
adonya_tellStraight:"Scylla was being held by League intelligence on Laicaille Habitat." (explain)
adonya_outA:"Wonderful. Well, I shouldn't take any more of your time..."
adonya_outB:"I need to get back to my $shipOrFleet. Goodbye."

text: "Oh... isn't that nice." She smiles. "You must be very successful."
You explain that you found Scylla on Laicalle Habitat and helped her initiate a very important project at the Galatia Academy.

With Zal's assistance, Scylla helped Provost Baird develop a method to open the Gate System.

It was a high-pressure project, and dangerous. Not for her, of course. Her job was on the theory and numbers side. Adonya nods, an extra wrinkle of concern on her forehead.

It didn't quite work out as expected, you explain, however you did manage to make history by using the so-called Janus Device to traverse the Gates, the first such trip in over 200 cycles. And it was made possible through Elissa Zal's engineering based on theoretical developments by Academician Scylla Coureuse.

"Oh!" Adonya lights up at your words. "I'm so happy she found Elissa again, what a nice girl. A little strange, though. Always up to something. But that's my Scylla."

"I think she gets her taste from me," Adonya says in a  conspiratorial half-whisper.

"You should have met her father! You'd have liked him, I think. Stars above, he could be a troublemaker. But he was fun," she nods to herself.
options:
adonya_tell4a:"And, um, that's all there is to tell."
adonya_tell4b:"And then Scylla and Zal disappeared. I don't know where to."
adonya_tell4c:"Then they disappeared together. I'm trying to find them."

text: "Oh, okay," she says.
"Oh, okay," she says, unconcerned.
"Oh," she says, apparently unconcerned. "Good luck."

"Thank you for stopping by, captain. I know you must be very busy and an old woman's worries are probably the last thing on your mind, but it was good of you to take the time."

She pauses for a moment. "If you find her, tell her she could send comms more often. I do so love hearing from her."

"Of course," she says. "Scylla is a good daughter."

"Sometimes it's a long time between them, and I get worried." She glances back toward her holotainment unit. "It'd be nice if she came for a visit."

"Now you be careful out there," she says. "I know you're young, but you're not immortal. And I heard on the holo all about these terrible pirates who..."

Her impression of what happens between the Fringe and Core is somehow both ridiculously exaggerated and entirely insufficient. The holo-networks of Fikenhild are famously more entertaining than informative.

Eventually you manage to extract yourself from the comms-link.
"I know, dear, you already mentioned it," she says.

"Of course it is," she says, humoring you. "It's always the fate of the worlds with Scylla, isn't it?"

"I know this is all very important to you, captain." Her voice takes on a more incisive tone. "But if she's hiding from someone, if she's hiding from you, I'm sure it's for a good reason. And like I told Mister Siyavong when he came by, I don't know where my daughter or Elissa are - and I wouldn't tell him if I did."

She crosses her arms.
